1:
On Matthew 26:1–2 — “When Jesus had finished all these sayings… Ye know that after two days is the feast of the passover, and the Son of man is betrayed to be crucified” — the witnesses mark the close of the great discourses and the deliberate step Christ takes toward his Passion.

The Latin fathers read the timing typologically. Hilary sees the Lord, having just foretold his return in splendour, now announce his suffering, “that they might learn the close connection between the sacrament of the Cross, and the glory of eternity.” Jerome and Remigius dwell on the name “Passover” (Hebrew Phase), drawing it not from the Greek for “to suffer” but from “to pass over” — the destroyer passing over the blood-marked doors (Exodus 12:13) — and find in it Christ’s own passing “out of the world to His Father.” Origen presses that it is “not ‘all’ barely, but ‘all these’” sayings that are finished, and hears in the very phrasing a deliberate movement toward the cross; Augustine harmonises the chronology with John’s “six days before the Passover” at Bethany (John 12:1).

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrinal point: this last prediction “clearly shows how willingly he offered himself to die… because God could not be appeased but by a sacrifice of obedience”; and Christ forewarns the disciples lest they be discouraged, thinking him “dragged to death by necessity.” The Puritan Henry observes that “while his enemies were preparing trouble for him, he was preparing himself and his followers for it,” and that after many distant notices “we still have need of fresh ones.”

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the day. Barnes and Clarke explain the feast as the memorial of Israel’s deliverance, the firstborn spared when Egypt’s perished (Exodus 12:1); Robertson, noting the futuristic present “cometh,” reckons this our Tuesday evening, the passover falling on the Jewish Friday. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley adds a detailed picture of the paschal ritual — the cup blessed and divided, the unleavened bread and bitter herbs, the child’s question (Exodus 12:26) — while the Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem and Leo strike the note all share: he “came to His passion willingly… lest the whole world should perish in its sins.”
2:
On Matthew 26:2 — “after two days is the feast of the passover, and the Son of man is betrayed to be crucified” — the witnesses weigh the reckoning of the days and the willingness it discloses.

The grammatical-historical commentators are occupied with the chronology and its bearing on the larger harmonistic question. Barnes and Clarke explain the feast itself: the destroying angel “passed over” the houses of Israel marked with blood while Egypt’s firstborn fell (Exodus 12:13), the one day of the Passover proper running on into the seven days of unleavened bread. Robertson, marking the futuristic present tenses “cometh” and “is delivered up,” sets the saying on the Tuesday evening (the beginning of the Jewish Wednesday), with the passover meal on the Thursday evening; Ellicott notes that the point of time, on the Jewish mode of counting, may be read either way (see John 13:1 on the disputed date).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley pauses over the manner of the feast, “which gives much light to several circumstances that follow” — the cup of wine solemnly blessed and divided (Luke 22:17), the supper begun with unleavened bread and bitter herbs, and the youngest present asking the reason of the solemnity (Exodus 12:26). Gill likewise places the words on the Tuesday, the passover beginning the following Thursday, a thing “well known to the disciples.”

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem and the Latin Leo lift the verse to its mystery. Cyril hears proof “that He came to His passion willingly” — unlike those who, not foreknowing, “die unwillingly,” Christ “spoke before of His passion” and “stedfastly set His face to go to Jerusalem,” and this “lest the whole world should perish in its sins.” Leo confesses the Easter festival “partakes of so sublime a mystery as to surpass… even the powers of great intellects,” yet one on which the Church “may not hold our peace.” So the verse joins a precise chronology to the deeper truth of a death freely chosen.

Gill adds that the day was “well known to the disciples, as it must be, since it always began on a certain day, the fourteenth of the month Nisan”; and Robertson notes that the same futuristic present (“is delivered up”) recurs through the narrative, marking events already set in motion.
3:
On Matthew 26:3–5 — the gathering of the chief priests and elders in the court of Caiaphas, their counsel “to take Jesus by subtilty, and kill him,” and their resolve “not on the feast day” — the witnesses read the hidden machinery of the Passion and the providence that overruled it.

The Latin fathers note the irony and the guilt. The Gloss says the Evangelist here lays bare “the hidden springs and machinery by which the Lord’s Passion was brought to pass.” Remigius marks that the higher the rank of those who “band together for any villainy, the greater the enormity”; Origen, that these were priests “not of the people of God, but… of Gomorrah,” plotting against “the firstborn of the whole creation” (Colossians 1:15). Chrysostom finds in the very multiplicity of “chief priests” — where the Law allowed but one for life — a sign that “the dissolution of the Jewish state was having its beginning.” Leo discerns providence: their caution “arose not from reverence for the festival, but from care for the success of their plot,” yet they could not succeed “till the days of” God’s appointing.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history. Barnes and Robertson identify the assembly as the Sanhedrin, met in the open court (aule) of the palace; Clarke and Robertson date Caiaphas (high priest A.D. 18–36) and his father-in-law Annas; Ellicott notes from John 11:49–50 that the plan was formed after the raising of Lazarus, the triumphal entry and the cleansing of the temple hastening it. Lightfoot adds the rabbinic memory of dark portents “forty years before the Temple was destroyed.”

The Reformed Calvin presses that the priests had resolved to “delay for a time,” so that Christ’s fixing of the day proves he spoke “by the spirit of prophecy” what “no man could have suspected.” On their “not on the feast day” he and Wesley draw the lesson that “contrary to their wish, God hastens the hour”: when Judas came they changed their purpose, “so the counsel of God took place, and the true paschal Lamb was offered up on the great day.”
4:
On Matthew 26:4 — “and consulted that they might take Jesus by subtilty, and kill him” — the witnesses dwell on the craft of the plot and the providence that frustrated its secrecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the strategy. Barnes notes that the council “did not dare to take him away openly, lest the people should rise and rescue him,” for Jesus was regarded by the multitude as a distinguished prophet, if not the Messiah; they knew he lodged by night outside the city. Robertson observes the aorist middle “they took counsel together” as marking “their puzzled state of mind” — they had no trouble finding Jesus (John 11:57), but how to seize him “by subtilty” was their problem, for “the Triumphal Entry and the Tuesday debate in the temple revealed the powerful following” he had among the Galilean crowds.

The Reformed Gill marks that the Jews had often attempted his life in vain, so that now they “meet and consult together, to form some scheme… in the most private manner they could.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke turns to the overruling hand of God: “the providence of God frustrated their artful machinations; and that event which they wished to conduct with the greatest privacy and silence was transacted with all possible celebrity, amidst the thousands who resorted to Jerusalem” for the feast. It was, he adds, “of the very first importance that the crucifixion of Christ… should” be done openly, before the great concourse, that the witnesses of the most essential act of redemption might be many. So the schools converge: the rulers’ cunning was real, but it served a counsel higher than their own.

Ellicott observes that the plan implied here and in “not on the feast day” was “hastened in its accomplishment by the unexpected treachery of Judas”: they had meant to wait till the feast was over, but the offered temptation was “too great to be resisted,” and they were content to risk “an uproar among the people.” Barnes adds that they knew Jesus lodged by night at Bethany, away from the city, which shaped their search for a secret seizure.
5:
On Matthew 26:5 — “But they said, Not on the feast day, lest there be an uproar among the people” — the witnesses agree that the rulers’ calculation was overturned by a higher purpose.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the fear. Barnes: the feast drew “a vast multitude… from all parts of Judea,” Jerusalem swelling (it was said) to millions, and amid such crowds “there were frequent tumults”; the Sanhedrin was “justly apprehensive” of sedition if they seized so popular a teacher openly. Robertson notes they reasoned the matter “must be postponed till after the feast… when the crowds had scattered,” that they might then catch him “by craft” as one would trap a wild beast. Clarke adds that they likely feared the Galileans, Jesus’ own countrymen.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the providence with force: “although those hungry dogs eagerly opened their mouths to devour Christ… God withheld them, by a secret restraint, from doing any thing by their deliberation or at their pleasure.” They resolve to delay, “but, contrary to their wish, God hastens the hour” — and this, he says, “is of great importance,” that we may know Christ was not overtaken by men’s plotting but offered at the appointed time. Gill notes that their resolve was itself contrary to their own rules for punishing a “presumptuous” offender.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sums the reversal: “This was the result of human wisdom. But when Judas came they changed their purpose. So the counsel of God took place, and the true paschal Lamb was offered up on the great day of the paschal solemnity.” The witnesses are thus of one mind: the men set the day by craft, but God set it by his decree.

Barnes notes the scale of the danger: at such times Jerusalem was said to hold near “three millions of people,” amid whom “there were frequent tumults and seditions,” so that an open arrest of so popular a teacher might well spark revolt. Calvin presses that it is “of great importance” to see Christ was not overtaken by men’s craft but offered at God’s appointed hour.
6:
On Matthew 26:6–7 — the supper in the house of Simon the leper at Bethany, and the woman with “an alabaster box of very precious ointment” who poured it on Jesus’ head — the witnesses give much labour to the identity of the woman and the meaning of the act.

The greatest debate among the fathers is whether the Gospels speak of one woman or several. Origen, with Chrysostom, judges that Matthew, Mark, and Luke describe one (or that Luke’s “sinner” differs), but that John’s Mary of Bethany is “another person… the sister of Lazarus”; Augustine likewise distinguishes Luke’s Simon from this one. Jerome insists the woman who anointed the head and she who washed the feet with tears were not the same. Ambrose allows it may have been “the same woman at two different times and two different stages of desert.” Chrysostom notes the Evangelist names Simon’s leprosy “to shew what gave this woman confidence” — seeing Jesus had cleansed the leper, she trusted he could “cleanse the uncleanness of her soul.” Hilary reads her as “the people of the Gentiles,” who give glory to God in Christ’s passion.

The grammatical-historical commentators settle the identity from John: this is Mary, sister of Lazarus (John 12:3). Barnes and Robertson explain the alabaster flask and the costly ointment (Mark’s “spikenard”); Lightfoot argues strenuously, from the head being anointed here and the feet in John, that “this supper in Bethany was the same with that mentioned” in John but “before the Passover.” Ellicott marks the “singular reticence” of the first two Evangelists in not naming her.

The Reformed Calvin notes the act “had happened a little before Christ came to Jerusalem, but is here introduced” to show what “suddenly drove the priests to make haste” — for Judas’s indignation at it furnished them their unlooked-for opportunity. The Puritan Henry hears in it “the singular kindness of a good woman to our Lord Jesus,” in the house of one whom Christ had likely cleansed of leprosy and “did not disdain to converse with.”

Robertson notes that the feast was given by Simon “who had been healed of his leprosy by Jesus… in honour of Jesus,” and that “all sorts of fantastic theories have arisen” over the woman’s identity. The fathers’ care to harmonise the four accounts — whether one woman or three or four — shows how seriously they took the unity and truth of the Gospel record.
7:
On Matthew 26:7 — “There came unto him a woman having an alabaster box of very precious ointment, and poured it on his head, as he sat at meat” — the witnesses describe the gift and again weigh who she was.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify her and the offering. Barnes and Ellicott name her, from John 12:3, as Mary the sister of Lazarus and Martha; the alabaster, Barnes explains, is “a species of marble… light, and of a beautiful white colour,” much used by the ancients for preserving costly ointment, and this ointment was “rare, and difficult to be obtained.” Robertson describes the flask, named from the Egyptian town of alabaster, and the “exceeding precious” perfume, its neck broken to pour it freely. Clarke surveys the dispute whether this is the act of John 12 or another, and Lightfoot presses the decisive point: “it was not the same supper with that in John 12… for then our Saviour’s feet were anointed, now his head.”

Lightfoot adds the custom behind the act: “Oil, perfumed with spices, was very usual in feasts, especially sacred; and it was wont to be poured upon the head of some one present” — citing the school of Shammai on the order of blessing oil and wine. The act, then, was no eccentric extravagance but a known mark of honour, here lavished without measure.

The Reformed Gill allows that “it is possible that one and the same woman might perform a like action at different times,” distinguishing this from the anointing in the house of Simon the Pharisee. The witnesses thus combine a careful identification of Mary, a precise account of the precious flask, and the recognition that her act drew on a familiar usage — raised here to an unrepeatable devotion poured upon her Lord’s head.

Lightfoot cites the school of Shammai on the feast-custom — the host “holds sweet oil in his right hand, and a cup of wine in his left” — to show the anointing drew on a known usage; Robertson describes the alabaster flask, “cylindrical… like a closed rosebud,” its slender neck broken that the whole precious contents might be poured at once.
8:
On Matthew 26:8 — “But when his disciples saw it, they had indignation, saying, To what purpose is this waste?” — the witnesses trace the murmur to its true source.

The grammatical-historical commentators narrow the blame. Barnes: John says it was Judas who voiced it, “but probably some of the others felt indignation,” while Judas alone “gave vent to his feelings” — for he “had the bag” (John 12:6) and “was a thief.” Robertson calls the word “waste” (apoleia) a reckoning of “dead loss… nothing but sentimental aroma,” “a cruel shock to Mary of Bethany”; Ellicott marks “the singular narrowing of the limits in the three narratives” — from “the disciples” to “some” to Judas alone (John 12:4) — and sees in the Traitor’s “narrow, covetous soul” one that could find in a lavish gift only matter for reproach. Clarke and Lightfoot note the Hebrew idiom by which the plural “disciples” may stand for the one who spoke.

The Reformed Calvin explains the contagion: “none of the others would ever have dared” to complain “if the wicked slander of Judas had not served for a torch to kindle them”; once he, “under a plausible pretext,” condemned the expense, the rest caught the murmur. Gill marks simply that they saw “what a costly box of ointment it was, and with what profusion she used it.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley agrees that “several of them were angry, and spoke, though none so warmly as Judas Iscariot.” So the witnesses set Mary’s extravagance of love against the Traitor’s extravagance of greed, and show how easily a specious complaint spreads from one false heart to many honest but mistaken ones.

Robertson observes that Matthew says only “for much” while Mark names “three hundred pence,” and that the comment was “a cruel shock to Mary of Bethany”; Ellicott finds “something almost terribly suggestive” in the way the narratives narrow from “the disciples” to the one covetous soul who first gave the murmur voice.

Clarke notes the Hebrew idiom by which “his disciples” may mean the one who spoke — as “the thieves also” (Matthew 27:44) means one, and “some doubted” (Matthew 28:17) means Thomas — though the murmur, once voiced, may indeed have been caught up by the rest.
9:
On Matthew 26:9 — “For this ointment might have been sold for much, and given to the poor” — the witnesses expose the pretext beneath the plea.

The grammatical-historical commentators reckon the sum and the hypocrisy. Barnes, Ellicott, and Gill note that Mark and John fix the value at “three hundred pence” (denarii) — near a labourer’s wages for a whole year (Matthew 20:2), Ellicott observes, enough to feed a multitude (John 6:7). Ellicott adds “the damning fact” that “the pretended zeal for the poor was the cloak for the irritation of disappointed greed… He was a thief, and bare the bag” (John 12:6). Lightfoot connects the murmur with the later supper, where the disciples thought Jesus bade Judas “give something to the poor” (John 13:29).

The grammatical-historical voices also defend Mary’s liberty. Barnes urges that the murmuring “was… without cause,” for “it was the property of Mary. She had a right to dispose of it as she pleased, answerable not to them, but to God.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the sharp moral that closes the matter: “How often does charity serve as a cloak for covetousness! God is sometimes robbed of his right under the pretense of devoting what is withheld to some charitable purpose, to which there was no intention ever to give it.” So the witnesses agree: the appeal to the poor was specious, the gift to Christ was just, and the heart that grudged it was already a thief’s.

Gill reckons the three hundred pence at near “nine pounds… very precious and costly ointment.” Lightfoot connects the cavil with the later supper, where, when Jesus said “What thou doest, do quickly,” some thought he bade Judas “give something to the poor” (John 13:29). Barnes presses that the murmuring was “without cause”: the ointment was Mary’s own property, and “she had a right to dispose of it as she pleased, answerable not to them, but to God.”

Barnes adds that the disciples “had no right over it, and no cause of complaint” had Mary given it all to Christ; the appeal to the poor, however plausible, could not make her costly love a “waste.”
10:
On Matthew 26:10 — “Why trouble ye the woman? for she hath wrought a good work upon me” — the witnesses hear Christ champion Mary against her critics.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the verb and the praise. Robertson notes the phrase “why do ye give trouble” (kopous parechete), a striking idiom: “Jesus champions Mary’s act… It is so hard for some people to allow others liberty for their own personalities to express themselves.” Clarke renders it “why do ye put the woman to pain?” — for “a generous mind is ever pained when… its proffered kindness is refused.” Ellicott observes that Mark’s word shows they were “bitterly reproaching her,” one after another, and that the “good work” is rather “a noble, an honourable work,” not the alms they preferred but a deed of love done upon Christ himself. Barnes adds that it was “done with a mind grateful, and full of love,” and was besides “preparative to his death” (v.12).

The Reformed Calvin meets the seeming difficulty that Christ, whose “whole life was a rule… of temperance and frugality, now approves of immoderate expense.” He answers carefully: Christ “does not maintain that the woman did right, in such a manner as if he wished that the same thing should be done every day,” but that “what she had done in a single instance was agreeable to God” for its particular reason — the honour of his approaching burial. Gill notes that Christ, knowing “the secret indignation… of their minds,” rose at once to her defence.

So the witnesses are agreed: the deed the disciples called “waste” the Lord calls a “good” — indeed a noble — work, justified not as a rule for daily imitation but as an honourable act of love rightly timed.

Gill notes that Christ knew their hearts both ways — “as man, partly by their looks… and partly by their words,” and, “as God,” the “secret indignation, and private resentment of their minds.” Robertson adds the abiding lesson: “it is easy to raise small objections to what we do not like” in others’ devotion, and hard “to allow others liberty for their own personalities to express themselves.”
11:
On Matthew 26:11 — “For ye have the poor always with you; but me ye have not always” — the witnesses balance the standing duty of charity against the unrepeatable occasion of honouring Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators guard both truths. Barnes: it was “a plain precept of religion” to regard the poor (Psalm 41:1; Proverbs 14:21; Galatians 2:10), and Christ “would not prohibit it”; but “every duty should be done in its place,” and the duty then incumbent was Mary’s — the poor they would have always, but his bodily presence only a little while. Clarke: “my bodily presence is about to be removed from you for ever… The woman, under a presentiment of my death, is preparing me for my burial.” Ellicott marks that the Lord met the objection “on their own ground,” not openly rebuking the dishonest objector, yet by “look and tone… ‘Me ye have not always’” making the Traitor feel he stood before One “who read the secrets of his heart.”

The Reformed Calvin draws a polemical caution. Christ “does not simply defend the anointing, so that we may imitate it, but assures us that it pleases God on some particular account.” This must be “carefully weighed,” he warns, lest we fall into “the error of contriving expensive modes of worshipping God, as the Papists do,” who from this text justify “incense, wax-tapers, splendid decorations, and pompous exhibitions.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley turns the providence kindly: “Such is the wise and gracious providence of God, that we may have always opportunities of relieving” the poor, “and so laying up for ourselves treasures in heaven.” So the witnesses keep the balance: charity is perpetual duty, but the honour paid to Christ in that hour was a duty of its own, never to return.

Barnes notes that Mark adds, “Whensoever ye will ye may do them good”: the poor would never cease out of the land (Deuteronomy 15:11), so the chance of charity would always remain, while the hour to honour Christ’s body for burial would not. The duty then incumbent, all agree, was precisely the one Mary had discharged.
12:
On Matthew 26:12 — “For in that she hath poured this ointment on my body, she did it for my burial” — the witnesses find in Mary an understanding the apostles lacked.

The grammatical-historical commentators read her insight. Robertson: “Mary alone had understood what Jesus had repeatedly said about his approaching death,” while the disciples, “wrapped up in their own notions of a political kingdom,” failed to sympathise with him as he faced the cross; “with the woman’s fine intuitions” she began to understand, and this was “her way of expressing her high emotions and loyalty” (the verb is that used of preparing a body for burial, John 19:40). Lightfoot is bolder: “She, and she first, believes that Christ should die; and, under that notion, she pours the ointment upon his head, as if… anointing it for burial.” Clarke notes the Septuagint use of the word for embalming (Genesis 50:2), and that Christ “took this opportunity to tell them… that he was shortly to die.” Ellicott observes the words “must have fallen with a strange sadness” on ears expecting the kingdom “immediately to appear.”

The Reformed Calvin adds the deeper figure: the ointment was valued by Christ not “on account of its odour, but solely in reference to his” burial — a symbol “that his grave would yield a sweet odour, as it breathed life and salvation through the whole world” (John 12:7). The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley grants that this “was not her design,” but “our Lord puts this construction upon it” to confirm what he had said of his approaching death.

So the witnesses honour Mary’s act as the one rightly-aimed devotion of that table: whether or not she fully grasped it, her gift became the embalming of a body soon to be given for the life of the world.

Ellicott observes that “the enthusiasm of the moment made them deaf to the real import of what they heard,” their Master alone among the company knowing what lay ahead; Robertson marks that Mary, “with the woman’s fine intuitions, did begin to understand,” and that her anointing “was her way of expressing her high emotions and loyalty” to the Christ who faced the cross.
13:
On Matthew 26:13 — “Wheresoever this gospel shall be preached in the whole world, there shall also this… be told for a memorial of her” — the witnesses hear a promise that is at once a tribute and a prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the foreknowledge it implies. Clarke calls it “another remarkable proof of the prescience of Christ”: a matter that “humanly speaking, depended on mere fortuitous circumstances” has yet stood “as firm and regular as the ordinances of heaven” — and as embalming preserves the body, so her deed should be preserved by the gospel’s telling. Ellicott marks that the prediction “tended… to bring about its own fulfilment,” yet the very prevision “of that universal extension” of the gospel, uttered at such a moment, ranks “among the proofs of a foreknowledge not less than divine”: “others saw victory only, and that immediate; He saw condemnation and shame and death, yet… through them a victory.” Barnes notes the deed is rightly recorded “as a memorial of her piety and self-denial.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson of judgment: the act “will do honour to” Mary “because it will be praised by the doctrine of the gospel.” Hence “we ought to estimate our works not by the opinion of men, but by the testimony of the word of God”; and in foretelling that “all nations, with one consent, will applaud this action,” Christ “indirectly censures his disciples,” whose own house had grudged it.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, fastening on the gospel that should be preached, turns to the eternal life it offers, “by faith” in the Son. The witnesses thus read the verse as honour to Mary, rebuke to her critics, and a quiet, sovereign forecast of the gospel’s spread through “the whole world” — carrying her memory with it.

Barnes adds that “the good deeds of the pious should be recorded and had in recollection,” so that her self-denial stands for ever as a memorial. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, dwelling on the gospel that should everywhere be preached, points to the eternal life it offers — “He that believeth on the Son hath eternal life” (John 6:47) — the very salvation in whose telling Mary’s deed would be carried to all nations.
14:
On Matthew 26:14–15 — “Then one of the twelve, called Judas Iscariot… What will ye give me, and I will deliver him unto you? And they covenanted… thirty pieces of silver” — the witnesses probe the motive and the meaning of the bargain.

The Latin fathers fasten on the word “then” and the freedom of the act. Chrysostom: “then,” when he heard the gospel should be preached everywhere — “for that made him afraid.” Raban: “he was neither compelled, nor invited, but of his own free will formed the wicked design.” Leo gives the classic anatomy of greed: “in comparison of the love of money all our affections are feeble… there is no trace of righteousness in that heart in which covetousness has once taken up its abode.” Jerome notes the wretch “does not demand any fixed sum… but… left it to those who bought” how much to give. Augustine and Origen find in the thirty pieces a dark fittingness — the price of him “who sought to redeem the whole world.”

The grammatical-historical commentators set the sum and the sign. Lightfoot and Wesley note that thirty pieces of silver was “the price of a slave” (Exodus 21:32); Ellicott adds that the priests, “scorning both his Master and himself, gauged their reward accordingly” (cf. Zechariah 11:12). Robertson sets the scene: “Mary and Judas extreme opposites: she freely spending in love, he willing to sell his Master for money” — and “her act of love provoked Judas to his despicable deed.” Barnes notes Luke’s word that “Satan entered into Judas” (Luke 22:3), tempting him through his “ruling passion” of avarice.

The Reformed Calvin marks the hardening: Christ’s admonition was “so far from… producing any change… that he immediately went away… to transact an infamous bargain.” The Puritan Henry calls it “the greatest unkindness” set hard upon “the greatest kindness,” aggravated in that the traitor was “one of the twelve.”

Origen finds a dark fittingness even in the sum — “thirty pieces of silver, as many… as the Saviour had dwelt years in the world” before his ministry — while Jerome contrasts the price with Joseph, sold by his brethren, observing that Judas “does not demand any fixed sum… but… left it to those who bought, to determine how much they would give,” as one delivering up a worthless slave.
15:
On Matthew 26:15 — “What will ye give me, and I will deliver him unto you? And they covenanted with him for thirty pieces of silver” — the witnesses weigh the price and the manner of the bargain.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the sum. Lightfoot, citing Maimonides, gives “the price of a slave… thirty selaim of pure silver” (Exodus 21:32), “whether the slave be worth a hundred pounds, or… one penny.” Clarke notes the textual reading “staters,” a stater being the shekel, “worth about 3s.… a goodly price for the Saviour of the world!” Ellicott reads the chief priests’ reckoning as scorn: “the reward was relatively a small one… the chief priests… saw through the sordid baseness of the man, and… gauged their reward accordingly” (cf. Zechariah 11:12). Barnes notes they “promised” the money but did not pay it then, lest he deceive them — the payment came after the deed (Matthew 27:3; Acts 1:18).

Robertson hears in Judas’s “What are ye willing to give me?” the very “aspect of the transaction” (Vincent): the colloquial “and I… will deliver him” (kago…parado&so) marks the cold initiative of the seller.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reckons the sum at “about three pounds fifteen shillings sterling… the price of a slave” (Exodus 21:32). The Reformed Gill identifies “them” as the chief priests to whom Judas came with his proposal. So the witnesses agree that the very figure — a slave’s purchase, foretold by Zechariah — measures both the Traitor’s baseness and the priests’ contempt, and that the appointed Scripture was thereby fulfilled to the letter.

Lightfoot, from Maimonides, gives the slave-price as “thirty selaim of pure silver” whatever the slave’s worth, a selaa weighing “three hundred and eighty-four barleycorns.” So the witnesses converge: the figure is no random bribe but the fixed valuation of a slave (Exodus 21:32), the very sum weighed out in Zechariah 11:12 — the prophets’ price of contempt, now paid for the Lord of glory and fulfilled to the letter.

Barnes notes that Mark and Luke say only that they promised him “money” — in the original, “silver” — while Matthew alone records the exact and ominous sum, the thirty pieces that Scripture had foretold.
16:
On Matthew 26:16 — “And from that time he sought opportunity to betray him” — the witnesses mark the settled, patient pursuit of the crime.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the diligence of sin. Robertson notes the imperfect tense, “sought opportunity” (ezetei eukairian) — “a good chance” — observing that “Judas went at his business and stuck to it.” Barnes and Gill add Luke’s qualification, “in the absence of the multitude” (Luke 22:6): the chief difficulty was to deliver him to the priests “so as not to have it known by the people… or so as not to excite tumult,” the very point on which Judas and the priests had agreed.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke turns the verse into a meditation on greed. The word means “a convenient or fit opportunity”; and “men seldom leave a crime imperfect: when once sin is conceived, it meets, in general, with few obstacles, till it brings forth death.” He breaks out, with Virgil, against “the love of money” — “how deceitful, how deeply damning” — quoting the heathen poet’s cry over a murder for gold, “to what dost thou not drive the hearts of men, O cursed hunger for gold?”

So the witnesses, though few here, are of one mind: the betrayal was no sudden passion but a fixed design, pursued with method and patience, seeking only the safe and secret moment — the working out of a sin already conceived in the heart, and now hunting its hour. Gill marks that, “prompted on by Satan, and the lucre of the money,” Judas waited for the most private occasion, when no rescue could be feared.

Gill adds that Judas sought to do it “in the most private manner… when he was alone… that no tumult might arise, and that there might be no danger of a rescue,” as he and the priests had agreed. Clarke breaks out, with Virgil’s cry over a murder for gold, against “the love of money” — “to what dost thou not drive the hearts of men?” — the working-out of a sin once conceived in the heart.
17:
On Matthew 26:17 — “Now the first day of the feast of unleavened bread the disciples came to Jesus, saying, Where wilt thou that we prepare for thee to eat the passover?” — the witnesses labour over the day and the great chronological question of the Last Supper.

The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the date. Clarke notes that, strictly, the feast of unleavened bread began on the fifteenth (Leviticus 23:5–6), but since the Jews began eating unleavened bread on the fourteenth (Exodus 12:18), “this day was often termed the first.” Robertson states the “famous controversy” on the apparent disagreement between the Synoptics and the Fourth Gospel (John 18:28); Ellicott holds that the precision of the first three Gospels “leaves no room for doubt that they looked on the Last Supper as the celebration of the actual Paschal Feast,” while John “leaves… a different impression.”

The Latin fathers read the transition from old to new. Remigius asks why, if the lamb typified Christ, he did not suffer on the night it was killed, and answers that “on this night He committed to His disciples the mysteries” of the new covenant. Chrysostom and John Chrysostom explain that the Jews “reckoned their day from the evening,” so “the first day” means the day on whose evening the passover was killed. Origen warns the Ebionites that we do not keep the Passover with “Jewish observances” but “celebrate spiritually” what they did in figure (Galatians 4:4); Hilary and Raban note the man’s name is withheld so that “all who would… receive Christ within the dwelling place of their own minds” may know the opportunity is theirs.

The Reformed Calvin grants the day “refers to the following day,” the killing being at hand. The Puritan Henry observes that Christ, “being made under the law, submitted to all the ordinances of it,” and died “the day after the passover.”

Augustine notes that Matthew’s “to such a man” is said “for brevity’s sake” — “no man speaks thus” in actual address — conveying that the disciples were sent to one particular person. Jerome fixes the day: “the fourteenth… when the lamb is killed, the moon is at full, and leaven is put away,” the New Testament here keeping “the practice of the Old.”
18:
On Matthew 26:18 — “Go into the city to such a man, and say… The Master saith, My time is at hand; I will keep the passover at thy house” — the witnesses read the quiet authority and foreknowledge of the command.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the curious phrase “such a man.” Robertson notes it is “the only instance in the N.T. of this old Attic idiom” (ton deina), the equivalent of “Mr. X,” and that Jesus may have named him, while Mark and Luke give the sign — “a man bearing a pitcher of water” (Mark 14:13; Luke 22:10) — whom the disciples were to follow. Ellicott and Barnes hold the master of the house “was probably a disciple, but secretly… for fear of the Jews,” and add that Luke names the two sent as Peter and John. Clarke and Robertson note that “My time is at hand” means “the time of my crucifixion.”

The Reformed Calvin reconciles the accounts: Matthew “specifies a certain man” while the others tell of a sign given “as to an unknown individual,” but “it cannot be doubted that, when they came to the house, they found that it was one of their acquaintances.” Christ, he adds, “enjoins him authoritatively to make ready a lodging” — a small but real mark of his lordship even as he goes to die.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the same phrase plainly: “The Master saith, My time is at hand — that is, the time of my suffering.” So the witnesses find in this brief errand both the homely detail of a borrowed upper room and the steady consciousness of the Lord, who orders the place of his last Passover even as his “time” presses upon him.

Gill describes the figure who would meet them — seemingly “a servant, that was sent” bearing a pitcher of water — while Clarke shows from Kypke that “my time” (kairos) means the appointed season of his suffering. Ellicott adds that the master of the house “was probably a disciple, but secretly… for fear of the Jews,” one of the many such believers in Jerusalem.
19:
On Matthew 26:19 — “And the disciples did as Jesus had appointed them; and they made ready the passover” — the witnesses note the obedience of the disciples and the substance of the preparation.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail what was done. Barnes: they “procured a paschal lamb… procured it to be killed and flayed by the priests, and the blood to be poured at the altar; they roasted the lamb, and prepared the bitter herbs, the sauce, and the unleavened bread.” Lightfoot, naming Peter and John as the two sent (Luke 22:8), lays out the rite step by step — the lamb “bought, approved” and slain — and shrewdly observes that they “only knew that Judas was about another business, while the rest supposed he was preparing necessaries.” Clarke adds, with Wakefield, that the Jews “considered the passover as a sacrificial rite,” Josephus calling it “a sacrifice.” Ellicott gathers the furnishing of the room, the couches, the lamb, bread, herbs, wine, and “conserve of sweet fruits.”

The Reformed Calvin makes the disciples’ compliance a pattern of faith. A doubt might have risen — in seeking an unknown man, whether they would obtain what they asked “while they were aware that everywhere he was… hated” — “yet they make no anxious inquiry about the result, but peaceably obey.” “If we are desirous to have our faith approved,” he adds, we must learn the like ready submission.

So the witnesses join a careful picture of the paschal preparation to a quiet commendation of obedience: the disciples ask where, are told, and simply do, leaving the result with their Master — even as one of their number is already about a darker errand.

Lightfoot lays out the steps the two must take — “a lamb was to be bought, approved” and slain — and notes shrewdly that they “only knew that Judas was about another business, while the rest supposed he was preparing necessaries.” Clarke adds, with Wakefield, that the Jews “considered the passover as a sacrificial rite,” Josephus calling it “a sacrifice,” so that the disciples’ errand was itself a priestly preparation.
20:
On Matthew 26:20 — “Now when the even was come, he sat down with the twelve” — the witnesses mark the hour, the posture, and the presence of the traitor among the Twelve.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time and manner. Barnes explains the lamb was killed “between the evenings” (Exodus 12:6), the supper eaten after sunset, often in the night (Exodus 12:8). Robertson: “he was reclining” (anekeito), lying on the left side, the right hand free — for though the first Passover was eaten standing (Exodus 12:11), later custom reclined. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic distinction between sitting and “lying… leaning on the left side upon couches.” Clarke discusses whether Christ kept the Passover some hours before the Jews, on “the same day… but not… the same hour.”

The Latin fathers dwell on Judas’s presence and Christ’s forbearance. Jerome: the Lord, having foretold his Passion, now foretells the traitor, “thus giving him place of repentance,” designating him “yet not by name.” Chrysostom: Christ declared the treachery “out of regard for him, and to bring him to a better mind,” and to comfort the disciples that he did not suffer “through weakness.” Leo: he met the wickedness “not… with a harsh and open rebuke, that penitence might find a readier way to one who had not been disgraced by public dismissal.” Remigius notes that “with the twelve” is said because Judas “was personally among them, though he had ceased to be so in merit.”

The Reformed Calvin reads the supper as the solemn close of the paschal rite, after which Christ “came to supper.” So the witnesses set the scene: the Lord reclines with the Twelve, the traitor among them, and meets the coming betrayal not with exposure but with a mercy that still leaves the door of repentance open.

Chrysostom adds that Christ declared the treachery “out of regard for” Judas, “to bring him to a better mind,” and to assure the rest he suffered not “through weakness”; Raban notes the homely detail that the “dish” (Matthew’s paropsis, Mark’s catinus) may have been “a square earthen vessel” into which the Lord and the traitor reached together, the exchange “not… overheard by the rest.”
21:
On Matthew 26:21 — “And as they did eat, he said, Verily I say unto you, that one of you shall betray me” — the witnesses feel the weight of betrayal at the sacred table.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene from the parallels. Barnes notes that John adds Jesus “was troubled in spirit, and testified” (John 13:21) — feeling deeply “the greatness of the crime that Judas was about to commit.” Robertson: “one of you” was “a bolt from the blue for all except Judas,” who was “startled to know that Jesus understood his treacherous bargain.” Clarke explains “shall betray me” as “will deliver me up,” for Judas “had already betrayed him” in the bargain and was now to deliver him over. Gill notes the discourse fell while they ate the lamb, the bread, and the bitter herbs, when much was customarily said of the feast’s meaning.

The Reformed Calvin presses the aggravation: to make “the treachery of Judas more detestable,” Christ points out “the aggravated baseness… that he was meditating the” betrayal of his own intimate friend while seated at the holy banquet. “If a stranger had done this, it would have been more easily endured; but that one of his intimate friends” should, “after having entered into an infamous bargain… be present at the sacred banquet, was incredibly monstrous.” Ellicott hears the words as “intentionally vague, as if to rouse” the others to self-questioning, for though guiltless of Judas’s crime they had “yielded to tendencies” — the murmuring, the quarrelling — “which were dragging them down to his level.”

So the witnesses gather the horror of the moment: at the table of fellowship, one heart already sold, the Lord announces the treachery plainly enough to wound, vaguely enough to summon every conscience to examine itself.

Gill notes it was “usual, whilst they were thus engaged” at the paschal table, “to discourse much about the reason and design of this institution,” so that the announcement broke into a holy conversation. Barnes adds from John that Jesus first “testified” — “bore witness” — having been “troubled in spirit,” feeling deeply “the greatness of the crime that Judas was about to commit.”
22:
On Matthew 26:22 — “And they were exceeding sorrowful, and began every one of them to say unto him, Lord, is it I?” — the witnesses find in the disciples’ grief and self-suspicion a mark of their innocence.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the scene. Barnes: John says “they looked on one another,” each — except Judas — “conscious of no such intention… beginning to examine himself.” This “showed their innocence, and their attachment to Jesus… that they were willing to know themselves; thus evincing the spirit of the true Christian.” Robertson notes the question’s form (meti ego eimi) “expects the answer No,” natural for all save Judas, “who had to bluff it out by the same form” (verse 25). Ellicott and Lightfoot draw on John’s fuller report (John 13:22) — the perplexed glances, Peter’s whisper to John, and the sign of the sop — all passing “as a by-play, unheard… by the other disciples.” Clarke marks that the eleven were truly sorrowful while “the hypocritical traitor… endeavored to put on the appearance of sorrow.”

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes their grief from mere panic: “I do not think that the disciples were alarmed, as persons struck with terror”; rather, “abhorring the crime, they are desirous to clear themselves from the suspicion of it.” It is “a mark of reverence” that, “when indirectly blamed, they do not reply angrily… but each person constitutes himself his own judge.”

So the witnesses commend the disciples’ response: not indignation at the charge nor accusation of one another, but sorrow and honest self-scrutiny — the proper instinct of those who fear above all to be found unfaithful to their Lord.

Lightfoot notes that at the supper of John 13 the Lord “had privately discovered the person to John only,” so that the table-question here belongs to a wider, more public moment. Barnes presses that their grief “showed how sensitive they were to the least suspicion of the kind,” and Robertson that the very form of the question expects the answer “No” — natural for all but the one who had to feign it.
23:
On Matthew 26:23 — “He that dippeth his hand with me in the dish, the same shall betray me” — the witnesses weigh whether this disclosed the traitor or only deepened the warning.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the dish and the act. Barnes and Clarke explain the “bitter sauce” (the charoseth), made of raisins, vinegar, and other seasoning, recalling the clay of Egyptian bondage, into which the bread was dipped; since families ate from several small dishes, “Judas reclined near” the Lord and dipped in the same one. Robertson: “they all dipped their hands, having no knives, forks, or spoons,” so the words mark “one of those who had dipped” — and “it is plain that Judas was not recognised by the rest.” Ellicott thinks the words were spoken “to a few only… nearest to their Master,” or Judas could scarcely have ventured his question of verse 25.

The Reformed Calvin holds that Christ “neither removes their doubt, nor points out the person of Judas, but only confirms… that” the traitor is one of them. Though it was hard to be left “in suspense and perplexity for a time,” this had its use: they were made to “contemplate the atrocity of the crime,” and afterwards saw “that the prediction of the psalm was fulfilled, He that ate pleasant food with me” has lifted his heel (cf. Psalm 41:9).

So the witnesses leave the disclosure veiled: the table-fellow who shares the dish is named as the betrayer, yet so generally that the eleven still cannot tell whom — the Scripture of the trusted friend turned enemy quietly fulfilling itself before their unseeing eyes.

Robertson describes the dish (tryblion) as “the platter with the broth of nuts and raisins and figs into which the bread was dipped before eating”; Clarke explains that families used “several little dishes… on different parts of the table,” so Judas’s dipping in the same one as the Lord marks him near at hand. Ellicott thinks the words were heard by “a few only… nearest to their Master,” else Judas could scarcely have ventured his question.
24:
On Matthew 26:24 — “The Son of man goeth as it is written of him: but woe unto that man by whom the Son of man is betrayed! it had been good for that man if he had not been born” — the witnesses hold together divine decree and human guilt.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the two clauses side by side. Barnes: “goeth” means “dies” (a Hebrew idiom, Genesis 15:2), “as it is written” in the Old Testament (compare Psalm 41:9 with John 13:18; see also Isaiah 53:4–9; Daniel 9:26); Luke says “as it was determined.” Ellicott calls this “the first direct reference of the coming passion… to the Scriptures,” and presses the balance: “it was appointed that the Christ should suffer, but that appointment did not make men less free agents, nor diminish the guilt” — as Peter too declares (Acts 1:16). Robertson: there are “some today who seek to palliate the crime of Judas,” but Jesus “here pronounces his terrible doom,” and Judas “heard it and went on.”

The Reformed Calvin meets the offence head-on: what could seem “more unreasonable than that the Son of God should be… betrayed by a disciple”? But Christ “declares that all this takes place only by the will of God,” proved “by the testimony of Scripture” — so that the decree does not excuse the deed.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley and Clarke fasten on the “woe.” Wesley applies it beyond Judas: “may not the same be said of every man that finally perishes?” — using it against universal salvation; Clarke and the Eastern Cyril keep the willing self-offering in view, that Christ “shunned not death… lest the whole world should perish.” So the witnesses confess both: the cross was written and determined, and the betrayer is justly woe-stricken.

Lightfoot notes that “it had been good for that man if he had not been born” was “a very usual way of speaking in the Talmudists” — “it were better for him that he were not created.” Clarke weighs the dreadful force of the doom against any thought that it could be said of one for whom there remained, after suffering, a release; and so reads the woe as final.
25:
On Matthew 26:25 — “Then Judas… said, Master, is it I? He said unto him, Thou hast said” — the witnesses are struck by the traitor’s effrontery and the searching reply.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the exchange. Barnes: “Thou hast said” means “thou hast said the truth. It is so. Thou art the man” (compare Matthew 26:64 with Mark 14:62). Clarke marks the impudence: “he knew, in his conscience, that he had already” struck the bargain, “and yet he says… Master, is it I?” — and observes the telling difference of address: the others said “Lord” (Kyrie), but “Judas dares not” and says only “Rabbi.” Ellicott connects the moment with John: “after the sop Satan entered into him” (John 13:27), and the answer “Thou hast said” “was not heard by all” (John 13:29).

The Reformed Calvin probes the psychology of hardened guilt: “though we often see persons trembling, who are conscious of doing wrong… there is mingled such stupidity, that they boldly make a flat denial.” Judas, “restrained by an evil conscience, cannot remain silent”; he is “tormented… and… overwhelmed with fear and anxiety, by that internal” executioner. Gill marks that he asked only after “all the rest had put the question.”

So the witnesses expose the traitor in his own words: the hypocrite who has already sold his Lord still dares the question, hoping his secrecy holds; and the Lord, with two words, lets him know that all is known — a mercy still, even now, had he repented.

Gill notes that Judas put his question only “after all the rest had put” theirs; Clarke marks the telling difference — “each of the other disciples said Kyrie, Lord, is it I? But Judas dares not” and says only “Rabbi.” Ellicott reads it as “reckless defiance” after “Satan entered into him”: did his Master indeed know his guilt? — yet the searching answer “Thou hast said” “was not heard by all,” only the command, “What thou doest, do quickly.”

Clarke marks the impudence the more: Judas asks “hoping that he had transacted his business so privately that it had not yet transpired,” as though the secrets of his heart were hidden from the One who read them through.
26:
On Matthew 26:26 — “Jesus took bread, and blessed it, and brake it… Take, eat; this is my body” — the witnesses gather at the institution of the Lord’s Supper, where the deepest division of the schools appears.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read it sacramentally and realistically. Jerome: when the typical Passover was ended, Christ came “to the true paschal sacrament,” offering, as Melchizedek had foreshadowed (Genesis 14:18), “the present verity of His Body and Blood.” The Gloss says he delivered his flesh and blood “under another kind… that faith might have its merit.” Augustine notes the disciples received not fasting, yet defends the Church’s later reverence “that the Lord’s Body should enter the Christian’s mouth before other food.” Chrysostom, with Leo and Hilary, fastens on Judas: “after the sop, Satan entered into him” (John 13:27), his sin aggravated that “he came near to these mysteries with such a heart.” Cyril of Jerusalem urges that the faithful are thereby “made of the same body and blood with Christ.”

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices read “this is” as “this signifies.” Wesley states it plainly: “This bread is, that is, signifies or represents my body, according to the style of the sacred writers” (Genesis 40:12; Galatians 4:24). Barnes agrees “this represents my body.” Robertson notes the bread was not “broken” as a symbol of his body (the genuine text of 1 Corinthians 11:24 omits “broken”), for his body was not broken (John 19:33). Calvin holds Christ “employed two symbols to show that our life is shut up in him,” supplying the place of meat and drink.

The Puritan Henry adds the timing — “as they were eating… at the latter end of the passover-supper” — the new ordinance coming “in the room of” the old, for “Christ our passover is sacrificed for us” (1 Corinthians 5:7). So the witnesses share one reverence for the mystery, while dividing over its mode — the fathers pressing a real participation in the Body, the Reformed a sign that seals the same union by faith.
27:
On Matthew 26:27 — “And he took the cup, and gave thanks, and gave it to them, saying, Drink ye all of it” — the witnesses dwell on the thanksgiving, the cup, and the scope of the blessing.

The Latin fathers draw out the mystery of the two kinds. Remigius marks that he gives “the cup of His Blood… shewing what joy He has in our salvation,” recalling “the blood of the covenant” (Exodus 24:8), and notes he says not “for a few, nor for all, but for many.” The Gloss explains that bread and wine are set apart “for representation of His Passion,” in which his blood was “separated from His Body.” Cyprian and Ambrose press the mingling of water with the wine — the water that flowed with the blood from his pierced side — while Jerome reads “the fruit of the vine” he will drink anew in the Father’s kingdom of the joy of redeemed Israel. Chrysostom: he “gave thanks… to instruct us… and shewed thereby that He came not to His Passion against His will.”

The grammatical-historical commentators set the cup in its paschal place. Barnes and Ellicott identify it with the cup of the Hallel (cf. v.30), taken “after supper” (1 Corinthians 11:25); Ellicott notes the later ritual passed the cup round thrice, one already in Luke 22:17. Clarke and Lightfoot detail the paschal cups and blessings.

The Reformed Calvin draws the pastoral aim: “to keep our faith wholly fixed on himself,” Christ “employed two symbols” — as the body needs meat and drink, so he “supplies the place of meat and drink,” giving “an astonishing display of his” grace. So the witnesses, whatever their difference over the mode, agree that the cup is given with thanksgiving, to be shared by all, as the seal of the blood poured out.

Augustine reads the promise to “drink it new with you” of “the immortality of renewed bodies,” the new wine answering to the old body given up in the Passion; Hilary infers from it that “Judas had not drunk with Him,” being unworthy “the communion of eternal sacraments,” while to all who partook he “promises… that they should drink with Him hereafter.”
28:
On Matthew 26:28 — “For this is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many for the remission of sins” — the witnesses expound the covenant, the textual question, and the scope of “many.”

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the words. Barnes: “this represents my blood”; Luke and Paul give “this cup is the new testament in my blood” (1 Corinthians 11:25), and “testament” should be rendered “covenant.” Robertson explains diatheke as covenant or will, operative at death (Hebrews 9:15–17); in Hebrew “to make a covenant was to cut up the sacrifice” (Genesis 15:9) — here the new covenant in Christ’s blood. Ellicott and Robertson note the best manuscripts omit “new” here, “probably introduced… to bring the text into harmony with St. Luke’s report.” Clarke warns against the popular error that “the New Testament” means the book, when it means the new covenant itself.

The Reformed Gill reads the cup as “an emblem… of his precious blood,” by which the new dispensation of the covenant of grace “was ratified and confirmed,” and all its blessings — “peace, pardon, righteousness, and eternal life” — conveyed; he points to the sprinkled blood of the first covenant (Exodus 24:8).

On the “many,” the Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the scope wide: “shed for many — as many as spring from Adam,” while the Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem finds in Psalm 23:5 (“Thou hast prepared a table before me”) the mystical table now set in place of the table of devils. So the witnesses confess one truth: the cup is the blood of the covenant, poured out unto the remission of sins.

Clarke warns against the popular error that mistakes “the New Testament” for the printed book, when it means “the new covenant” itself; Robertson adds that diatheke serves for both covenant and will, becoming “operative at death” (Hebrews 9:15–17). So the cup is the seal of a covenant cut, like the ancient ones, in sacrificial blood — here the blood of Christ, poured out for the remission of sins.

Barnes adds that “by this cup” Christ “meant the wine in the cup, and not the cup itself” — pointing to it, “This wine represents my blood about to be shed” — the symbol of the covenant now sealed.
29:
On Matthew 26:29 — “I will not drink henceforth of this fruit of the vine, until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father’s kingdom” — the witnesses read the farewell and the promise of the great reunion.

The grammatical-historical commentators take the words first plainly, then forward. Barnes: this marks that “the observance of the passover, and of the rites shadowing forth future things, here end”; “I am about to die… This is the last time that I shall partake of them with you,” until “my Father’s kingdom is established” and they share “the blessings and triumphs” the types foretold. Clarke renders it “wine of a widely different nature… which the kingdom of God shall produce.” Ellicott prefers “I shall not drink,” as accepting what God ordained, and reads the “new” wine of “the kingdom of God, completed and perfected.” Robertson cautions against pressing “fruit of the vine” literally: in the Messianic banquet the language “does not make it obligatory to employ” literal wine.

The Reformed Calvin notes the disciples were thereby “warned both of their Master’s” departure and of the hope beyond; he thinks it “of little importance… at what precise moment Christ said this.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sums it warmly: “I shall taste no more wine, till I drink wine of quite another kind in the glorious kingdom of my Father. And of this you shall also partake with me.” So the witnesses hear in the verse both a solemn parting — the last cup on earth — and a sure pledge of the table in the kingdom, where the broken fellowship of this night shall be restored and perfected.

Barnes reads the “new” drinking of “the blessings and triumphs of the redeemed in heaven,” the reality the paschal cup foreshadowed; Robertson, citing Deissmann’s papyri, notes that “fruit of the vine” need not be pressed literally for the Messianic banquet. So the parting word is also a betrothal — the last earthly cup pledging a feast in the perfected kingdom.

Gill infers from the words that Christ “had drank of the cup” himself, since “none might eat till he that blessed and brake the bread had tasted of it”; the farewell is thus also a fellowship, broken now but pledged again.
30:
On Matthew 26:30 — “And when they had sung an hymn, they went out into the mount of Olives” — the witnesses identify the hymn and read the move toward Gethsemane.

The grammatical-historical commentators agree on the song. Gill, Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley identify the hymn as the Hallel, Psalms 113–118, sung in two parts at the Passover, “by the universal consent of Jewish antiquity.” Robertson notes that they did not go out at once: Jesus tarried for the discourse and prayer of John 14–17, perhaps going out to the street after John 14:31, since it was no longer obligatory to remain indoors till morning (Exodus 12:22). Wesley adds that Olivet was “about two miles from Jerusalem.”

The Latin fathers moralise the singing and the ascent. Chrysostom rebukes those who rise from the table “drunk… when they should have given thanks, and closed with a hymn”; the last prayer of the mysteries, he says, answers to this hymn. Jerome bids whoever “is filled… upon the bread and cup of Christ” praise God and “ascend the Mount of Olives,” where is “refreshment after toil.” Origen: they go “from height to height, because the believer can do nought in the valley.” Raban relates the hymn to the thanksgiving of John 17, and to the Psalm, “The poor shall eat and be filled, they shall praise the Lord” (Psalm 22:26).

Several voices already look to the prophecy soon to be cited — Hilary marks Christ’s prediction supported by “old prophecy, I will smite the shepherd” (Zechariah 13:7). So the witnesses join a precise account of the paschal Hallel to a meditation on praise that rises, even on the night of betrayal, before the descent into the garden of agony.

Chrysostom turns the verse to reproof: “Let them hear who will not tarry for the final prayer in the sacred mysteries; for the last prayer… represents that hymn” — Christ gave thanks before, and sang after, “that we also should do” so. Jerome marks the emphatic “this night,” the hour of trial, that the disciples, forewarned, “might not… despair of salvation,” but, “doing penitence,” be restored.
31:
On Matthew 26:31 — “All ye shall be offended because of me this night: for it is written, I will smite the shepherd, and the sheep… shall be scattered” — the witnesses read the forewarning of the disciples’ fall.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the word and the prophecy. Clarke renders “ye shall be offended” as “ye will all be stumbled… forsake me, and lose… your confidence”; as a smitten shepherd leaves the flock scattered and exposed. Ellicott calls the citation of Zechariah 13:7 “every way suggestive,” showing that the Lord’s thoughts “had dwelt… on that chapter,” and that the saying corresponds in substance with John 16:32. Barnes notes the occasion: Peter’s bold claim that he would die with him (John 13:37).

The Puritan Henry sees “a dismal scattering storm just arising”: they would be “so frightened with the sufferings, that they would not have the courage to cleave to him.”

The Reformed Calvin notes that what Matthew and Mark extend to all the disciples, Luke relates as spoken to Peter; but “it is probable that Christ spoke to them in the person of one man, who was to be admonished more than all the rest,” and who would need “extraordinary consolation, that, after having denied Christ, he might not be altogether overwhelmed with despair.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads it plainly: “something will happen to me, which will occasion your falling into sin by forsaking me” (Zechariah 13:7). So the witnesses hear a tender warning: the shepherd struck, the flock scattered — foretold not to crush but to forearm them against despair.

Clarke develops the figure: the shepherd smitten, “the leader and guardian being removed, the whole flock shall be scattered, and be on the point of becoming a prey to ravenous beasts.” Ellicott marks that the Lord not only cited but “led the disciples to dwell on” the chapter of Zechariah 13:7, where the smiting of the shepherd is bound to the refining of the flock.

Gill notes the saying came “either before they went out of the house… or as they were going along” to Olivet — a word spoken on the very road into the night of trial.
32:
On Matthew 26:32 — “But after I am risen again, I will go before you into Galilee” — the witnesses hear, in the midst of the warning, a word of comfort and promise.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as both consolation and appointment. Barnes: the promise was given “to encourage and support them,” and as “an indication where he might be found” — he would “meet them all in Galilee,” as he did (Mark 16:7; Matthew 28:16). Clarke keeps the shepherd-figure running: “though the shepherd has been smitten and the sheep scattered, the shepherd shall revive… collect the scattered flock, and go before them.” Ellicott notes soberly that these words “appear to have fallen at the time unheeded… and to have been rapidly forgotten,” for “no expectation of a resurrection is traceable in their after conduct.”

The Reformed Gill draws the pastoral comfort: though the shepherd be smitten and the sheep scattered, he will rise, “appear to them,” and “go before them, as a shepherd” before his flock. Calvin reads it with the prophecy of Zechariah: after foretelling the scattering, the prophet treats “of the restoration of the Church,” lest the godly be “overwhelmed with despair” — for God “does not cease to recognise as his those who are driven out and scattered” for a time.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley puts it tenderly: “Though you forsake me, I will not for this forsake you.” So the witnesses find the promise lodged exactly where it is most needed — between the prophecy of scattering and its fulfilment — assuring the failing disciples of a Shepherd who rises and gathers his own again.

Ellicott notes soberly that the promise “appear[s] to have fallen at the time unheeded… and to have been rapidly forgotten,” for “no expectation of a resurrection is traceable in their after conduct.” Calvin draws the comfort from Zechariah’s sequence — after the scattering comes “the restoration of the Church” — that God “does not cease to recognise as his those who are driven out and scattered” for a season.

Gill adds that the scattered sheep should be “gathered together again by him, their good shepherd,” who would after his rising “appear to them, be at the head of them, and go before them.”
33:
On Matthew 26:33 — “Peter answered… Though all men shall be offended because of thee, yet will I never be offended” — the witnesses read Peter’s well-meant but presumptuous boast.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both the love and the self-reliance. Barnes: the word “men” is wrongly inserted — Peter meant “the disciples”; his confidence was “entirely characteristic,” sprung “from true love to Jesus,” but also “from too much reliance on his own strength” and “ignorance of himself.” Robertson: “made to stumble, not offended” (volitive future), and observes Peter “ignored the prophecy of the resurrection… intent on showing that he was superior to ‘all’ the rest.” Ellicott notes the harmonistic difficulty — Matthew and Mark place the boast after leaving the room, Luke and John before — and judges it “an example of the natural dislocation” of the order of rapid events.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke turns it into a portrait of presumption: “The presumptuous person imagines he can do every thing, and can do nothing… promises every thing, and performs nothing”; “there is nothing we know so little of as ourselves.”

The Reformed Calvin grants Peter “uses no hypocrisy, but speaks with sincere affection,” yet “a false confidence in his virtue carries him away into foolish boasting,” and he is “justly reproved… because he had not been sufficiently careful to examine himself.” The event showed he “promised more for himself than he was able to accomplish.” So the witnesses agree: Peter’s love was real, but his self-confidence blind — a warning that the warmest zeal, untaught by self-knowledge, is no guard against falling.

Clarke draws the maxim: “The presumptuous person imagines he can do every thing, and can do nothing… the strength of pride is only for a moment.” Barnes traces the boast to three roots — “true love to Jesus,” “too much reliance on his own strength,” and “ignorance of himself” — a warning that warm affection, untaught by self-knowledge, is no safeguard against falling.

Gill notes that Peter, “always a forward man, free to speak his mind,” was “often the mouth of the rest,” so that his boast spoke for more than himself even as it singled him out.
34:
On Matthew 26:34 — “Verily I say unto thee, That this night, before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice” — the witnesses mark the solemnity and exactness of the prediction.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time and the certainty. Barnes explains that Mark and Luke add “before the cock crow twice”: the cock crows about midnight and again at break of day, the latter being the “cock-crowing” familiarly so called (Mark 13:35). Lightfoot reads “before the cock crow” as “within the time of cock-crowing,” reconciling it with John 13:38. Clarke notes the force of the warning: not a gradual falling away, “but thou wilt do it this very night,” and not merely forsake but “deny that thou hast… any knowledge of… me.” Ellicott urges that the agreement of all four Evangelists places the prediction “beyond the shadow of a doubt,” and that the prevision is “essentially superhuman.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley dates it: “before three in the morning, the usual time of cock crowing,” though “one cock was heard to crow once, after Peter’s first denial.”

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, on the same theme of Christ’s foreknowledge, turns to the penitent thief who was promised, “Verily… This day shalt thou be with me” (Luke 23:43) — a like “Verily I say unto thee” of sovereign certainty. So the witnesses dwell on the precision of the word: the very night, the very number, the very signal — a foresight that marks the speaker as more than man, and that, fulfilled, would one day break Peter’s heart to a saving repentance.

Gill notes the solemn “verily,” Christ’s “Amen,” prefixed “as being a certain truth”; Lightfoot reconciles the accounts by reading “before the cock crow” as “within the time of cock-crowing” (John 13:38). Ellicott reminds us that to the sin thus foretold “was attached the penalty” of one who should deny the Son before men — making the prevision the more searching, and the later look of Christ the more piercing.

Clarke presses the sharpness of the warning: not merely that Peter would forsake him, but that he would “deny that thou hast, or ever had, any knowledge of… me; and this… this very night.”
35:
On Matthew 26:35 — “Peter said… Though I should die with thee, yet will I not deny thee. Likewise also said all the disciples” — the witnesses note the redoubled boast and the Lord’s gentleness.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the vehemence. Gill, with Mark, says Peter “spake the more vehemently,” his spirit raised “to a greater pitch… in stronger terms.” Robertson notes the strong negation (ou me se aparnesomai) and that this was “a noble speech and meant well,” though Peter’s boast “is made still stronger” and “the other disciples were undoubtedly embarrassed… and lightheartedly joined in the same profession.” Barnes: all said it, yet all “fled at the approach of danger… practically denying that they knew him” (Matthew 26:56). Clarke: Peter “does not take the warning… he trusts in the warm, sincere attachment… which he now feels, not considering that this must speedily fail, unless supported by the power of God.”

Ellicott observes that Peter “was not alone” in the resolve: Thomas had spoken like words before (John 11:16), and all “felt as if they were prepared to face death.” To them Christ had been not only “righteous,” but “good” and kind, and so “for Him they even dared to die” (Romans 5:7).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley marks the Lord’s forbearance: “such was the tenderness of our Lord, that he would not aggravate their sin by making any reply.” So the witnesses set the disciples’ sincere but overconfident vow against their coming flight, and against the silent gentleness of the Master who knew what the night would bring.

Gill notes that Peter, as Mark says, “spake the more vehemently,” his spirit raised “to a greater pitch… in stronger terms”; Barnes marks the issue — all said the same, yet all “fled at the approach of danger… practically denying that they knew him” (Matthew 26:56). So the witnesses set the loud, sincere vow of the whole company against the silence into which their courage would so soon collapse.

Barnes notes that to “deny” was to disown “my connexion with thee, or that I knew thee” — the very thing all the disciples, vowing the contrary, would shortly do.
36:
On Matthew 26:36 — “Then cometh Jesus with them unto a place called Gethsemane, and saith… Sit ye here, while I go and pray yonder” — the witnesses enter “the bloody scene” of the agony, and the fathers defend the reality of Christ’s suffering.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place. Robertson and Barnes explain Gethsemane (“oil-press”) as an enclosed olive orchard beyond the brook Kidron at the foot of Olivet (John 18:1), where ancient olives still stand; Clarke derives the name from “press” and “oil.” Calvin notes from Luke that Christ came there “as he was wont” — not to hide, but, “as if he had made an assignation with his enemies,” to present himself to death (John 18:2), a further proof of his obedience.

The Latin and Eastern fathers guard the truth of his sorrow against the heretics. Hilary reports that some read his being “sorrowful and very heavy” as proof that “fear of death assailed the Son of God,” and so that he was a creature made from nothing — an error he resists. Ambrose answers finely: “He is sorrowful, yet not Himself, but His soul… for He took upon Him my soul, and my body.” Jerome: the impassible Deity remained, while “the Son of God suffered… actually… in respect of that part of Him which could suffer” — the assumed humanity. Augustine appeals to the Evangelists as “witnesses that He had a” true body and soul. Chrysostom and John Chrysostom note he prayed apart, “teaching us to study quiet and retirement for our prayers,” and took the three lest the rest “be cast down.”

The Puritan Henry calls this “the beginning of sorrows”: “now the sword of the Lord began to awake against the man that was his Fellow.” So the witnesses enter the garden confessing a real, not feigned, anguish — the true Son of God suffering in a true human soul, and seeking, even there, the place and the company of prayer.

Jerome notes that Gethsemane “is interpreted, The rich valley,” where the Lord “prayed alone for all”; Origen hears in “My soul is sorrowful even unto death” a sorrow whose “beginnings only are in me” — not to endure for ever, but to be ended when, dying for sin, he should “die also to all sorrow.”
37:
On Matthew 26:37 — “And he took with him Peter and the two sons of Zebedee, and began to be sorrowful and very heavy” — the witnesses dwell on the chosen three and the depth of the sorrow.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the privilege and the weight of grief. Barnes and Robertson observe that Peter, James, and John — the same favoured at the raising of Jairus’s daughter (Luke 8:51) and the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1) — are again “chosen out of the chosen,” taken along (Robertson) “to share the agony of soul… to give him some human sympathy.” The word for “very heavy,” Barnes says, “is much stronger” than “sorrowful”; Clarke renders the verbs as “exquisite sorrow, such as dissolves the natural vigour, and threatens to separate soul” from body.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley probes the cause as far as reverence allows: the anguish came “probably from feeling the arrows of the Almighty stick fast in his soul, while God laid on him the iniquities of us all. Who can tell what painful and dreadful sensations were then impressed on him by the immediate hand of God?” — the word signifying “to be penetrated with the most exquisite sorrow.”

The Reformed Calvin finds here “the true test of virtue”: as Christ now “fights face to face with temptation,” “the weakness of the flesh, which was formerly concealed, shows itself.” God had tried his Son by “preparatory exercises,” but now brings the great contest. The Latin Clarke adds that the three were taken “that they might contemplate this agony in the light of that glory which they had… seen” on the mount. So the witnesses gaze on a sorrow real and crushing — the Redeemer, with his nearest friends beside him, beginning to bear the weight of the world’s sin.

Gill adds that the three were chosen as “the strongest, and best able to bear the shocking sight,” his “favourite disciples,” and the same who had witnessed his glory on the mount — “very proper persons to be witnesses” now of his abasement, that the one sight might interpret the other.
38:
On Matthew 26:38 — “Then saith he unto them, My soul is exceeding sorrowful, even unto death: tarry ye here, and watch with me” — the witnesses measure the depth of the agony and the craving for sympathy.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the words. Barnes: “his human nature — his soul — was much and deeply affected”; “even unto death” denotes “extreme sorrow and agony,” the sufferings of death “put for extreme and indescribable anguish” — “my sorrows are so great, that under their burden I am ready to die.” Clarke renders it “surrounded with exceeding sorrow… My soul is so dissolved in sorrow… that, if speedy succour be not given to my body, death must be the” result. Robertson explains “watch with me” (gregoreite) as “keep awake”: Jesus “pleaded for a bit of human sympathy as he wrestled with his Father… It did not seem too much to ask.” Ellicott: “the infinite sadness of that hour leads the Master to crave for sympathy from the three.”
