The Reformed Calvin says Christ “communicates to them his” sorrow “in order to arouse them to sympathy… that they might afterwards be more ashamed of their carelessness.” The phrase “expresses a deadly wound of grief; as if he had said, that he fainted, or was half-dead” — comparing Jonah’s reply, “sorrowful… even unto death” (Jonah 4:9). He notes that “some of the ancient writers” handled the passage with a misplaced fear of admitting Christ’s true grief.

So the witnesses, closing this unit at the threshold of the prayer, behold the Lord in real and overwhelming sorrow — not feigned, not slight, but “even unto death” — asking of his friends only that they keep awake and watch beside him in his hour.

Ellicott marks that, if the three may not fully see or hear the throes of that agony, “it will be something to know that they are at least watching with Him, sharers in that awful vigil”; and Robertson, that the plea to “keep awake” beside him in his wrestling “did not seem too much to ask” of friends who had just vowed to die with him.
39:
"O my Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me: nevertheless not as I will, but as thou wilt." This is the heart of Gethsemane, and the witnesses read it as the supreme proof of Christ's true humanity and the harmony of his two wills with the Father's. The Latin and Alexandrian fathers guard the mystery. Origen: he prays "not according to His Divine and impassible Substance, but according to His human and weak nature," for "in taking upon Him the nature of human flesh, He fulfilled all the properties thereof." Augustine reads it as our pattern: "Man, behold thyself in Me, that thou canst will somewhat apart of thyself," yet subordinate it — "Nevertheless not as I will, but as thou wilt." Hilary offers a distinctive reading: Christ prays not from fear, but that the cup "as it is drunk by Me, so let it be drunk by these," his disciples, "without fear of death." Leo makes the words "the health of the whole body... instruction to the faithful, [which] crowns the martyr."

The grammatical-historical Barnes states the doctrine of the atonement: "the fact that these sufferings were not removed... shows that it was not consistent with the justice of God... that men should be saved without the awful sufferings of such an atonement." When Jesus "speaks of his will, he expresses what human nature, in view of such great sufferings, would desire." Ellicott connects it to "He learned obedience by the things that He suffered" (Hebrews 5:8); the prayer "If it be possible" recognizes "the natural, necessary limits of our Lord's humanity." Robertson: "instantly surrendered his will to the Father's will and drank it to the full... Here Jesus won the power to go on to Calvary."

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest treatment, against the Monothelite heresy. Christ's prayer is genuine, though "abrupt," like "a vessel tossed by tempests"; "the strength and violence of grief suddenly drew this word from his mouth, to which he immediately added a correction." Against those who deny Christ feared, Calvin points to the bloody sweat (Luke 22:44): "that mortal sweat could only have proceeded from fearful and unusual horror," when "he saw the wrath of God exhibited to him... charged with the sins of the whole world." Yet in him "the two wills" of God and man "differed from each other without any conflict," like discordant musical notes producing harmony. Wesley: "he was heard in that which he feared. God did take away the terror and severity of that inward conflict." The witnesses agree: here the Son of God, truly man, shrinks from death yet yields perfectly to the Father.
40:
"And he cometh unto the disciples, and findeth them asleep, and saith unto Peter, What, could ye not watch with me one hour?" The witnesses note the tender disappointment of Christ and the surprising cause of the disciples' sleep. The grammatical-historical Barnes corrects a common misreading: the sleep was "just the reverse" of stupid indifference; "it was proof of their great attachment, and their deep sympathy," for Luke says they slept "for sorrow." He cites medical testimony that "profound sleep" follows great grief, even in mothers at a child's death and criminals before execution. Ellicott adds that "it was now near the dawning of the day, and their eyes had not closed in sleep for four-and-twenty hours."

The reproof falls on Peter especially. Clarke: "Is this the way you testify your affectionate attachment to me? Ye all said you were ready to die with me; what, then, cannot you watch one hour?" — noting a manuscript variant making the reproach singular to Peter, "couldst thou not." Ellicott observes Mark individualizes it: "Simon, sleepest thou?" — "He had boasted of his readiness to do great things. He could not so much as rouse himself to watch for one hour." Robertson hears "a tone of sad disappointment... Every word struck home," and notes "one hour" measures "the time that had passed since their Master had left them."

The Reformed Calvin draws the pastoral lesson on prayer's rhythm: "believers are not required to be so constant in prayer as never to cease... following the example of Christ, they continue their prayers till they have proceeded as far as their infirmity allows, then cease for a short time, and immediately after drawing breath return to God." He feels the weight of the disciples' failure: "it was a bitter aggravation of his sufferings, that even they forsook him," for "though he did not need the assistance of any one, yet as he had voluntarily taken upon him our infirmities... the indifference of those whom he had selected to be his companions added a heavy and distressing burden to his grief."

The witnesses agree on the verse's poignancy: Christ, in his deepest agony, sought the sympathy of his closest friends and found them asleep — not from contempt but from a sorrow that overwhelmed them. The gentle question to Peter, who had so loudly boasted (v.33), is both a reproof and a warning of the greater failure soon to come. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment attached here belongs to the woes of chapter 23, not this verse.)
41:
"Watch and pray, that ye enter not into temptation: the spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak." The witnesses receive this as a gracious warning and a tender apology for the sleeping disciples. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the "temptation" as the coming trial of their faith — the rejection and death of Jesus — which "would try their faith, because... they had cherished the belief that he was to establish a kingdom while he lived." The exhortation was "not intended for an apology for their sleeping, but to excite them... to be on guard, lest the weakness of human nature should be insufficient." Ellicott notes "watch" became "the watchword of the early disciples" (1 Corinthians 16:13; Colossians 4:2; 1 Peter 5:8), leaving its mark in the names "Gregory" and "Vigilantius, the watcher." He hears "a tenderness in the warning": the Master "recognises the element of good, their readiness to go with Him to prison or to death," even as the flesh and spirit war (Galatians 5:17).

Clarke draws the practical sense: "If ye cannot endure a little fatigue when there is no suffering, how will ye do when the temptation... cometh? Watch — that ye be not taken unawares; and pray — that when it comes ye may be enabled to bear it." Robertson connects it to the Lord's Prayer: "Thus we are to understand the prayer in Matthew 6:13 about leading (being led) into temptation." Wesley calls it "how gentle a rebuke... and how kind an apology! especially at a time when our Lord's own mind was so weighed down with sorrow."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of watchful prayer from weakness: "the manner of resistance which is here enjoined is, not to draw courage from reliance on our own strength... but, on the contrary, from a conviction of our weakness, to ask arms and strength from the Lord." He notes the balance: Christ "gives them the praise of [a willing spirit], in order that their weakness may not throw them into despair, and yet urges them to [pray], because they are not sufficiently endued with the power of the Spirit." The "general rule" is that "it is our duty to keep diligent watch by prayer; for we do not yet possess the power... in such a measure as not to fall frequently through the weakness of the flesh." Yet there is comfort: Christ "promises that all who, being earnest in prayer, shall perseveringly oppose the slothfulness of the flesh, will be victorious." The witnesses agree: the willing spirit needs the help of watchful prayer, because the flesh, left to itself, will surely fail.
42:
"O my Father, if this cup may not pass away from me, except I drink it, thy will be done." The witnesses mark a deepening of the prayer — a fuller acceptance of the cup that cannot pass. Ellicott notes "a slight change of tone": "it is as though the conviction that it was not possible that the cup could pass away from Him had come with fuller clearness before His mind, and He was learning to accept it. He finds the answer to the former prayer in the continuance, not the removal, of the bitter agony." He places here Luke's "great drops of blood" and the strengthening angel (Luke 22:43-44). The Reformed Calvin agrees: Christ "no longer asks to have [the cup removed] but, leaving out this prayer, insists rather on obeying the purpose of God"; "the first encounter with temptation animated him to approach death with greater confidence."

Barnes devotes a long meditation to the bloody sweat and the strengthening angel, defending the fact against skeptics with parallels from Aristotle, Diodorus, and Voltaire's account of Charles IX, whose "blood oozed out of all his pores." He explains why "the Divine nature did not minister strength to the human": Christ came "to be a perfect example of a holy man," submitting "to the common conditions of humanity... that he should suffer as other men, and be strengthened as other men" (Hebrews 1:14). On the cause of the agony, he wisely declines to dogmatize: "Where the Scriptures are silent about the cause, it does not become us confidently to express an opinion" — perhaps "a rush of feeling from every quarter," the concentrated "sufferings of the atonement pouring together upon him."

Clarke fixes on the words as proof of the atonement's reality: "it was his humanity alone that could suffer; and if it did not appear that he had felt these sufferings, it would have been a presumption that he had not suffered, and consequently made no atonement. And had he not appeared to have been perfectly resigned... his sacrifice could not have been a free-will but a constrained offering."

The witnesses agree: the second prayer shows the victory being won — fear subdued, the will yielded, the cup accepted. The deepening from "let it pass" (v.39) to "if it may not pass... thy will be done" traces, in the very words of Christ, the progress of his obedience, the genuine struggle of a human nature shrinking from death yet bringing itself, by prayer and the Father's strengthening, to drink the cup of the world's redemption willingly and to the dregs.
43:
"And he came and found them asleep again: for their eyes were heavy." The brief verse draws short comment. Gill notes they "were aroused and awaked, in some measure, by what he had said to them; but no sooner was he gone but they fell asleep again." Robertson parses the periphrastic past perfect: "Their eyes had been weighted down with sleep and still were, as they had been on the Mount of Transfiguration" (Luke 9:32) — linking this drowsiness to the disciples' failure at that earlier hour of glory.

Ellicott reads the motive of Christ's return tenderly: "the craving for human sympathy in that hour of awful agony. He does not now rouse them or speak to them. He looks on them sorrowfully, and they meet His gaze with bewildered and stupefied astonishment. They wist not what to answer Him" (Mark 14:40). Clarke raises a question others leave unasked: "Was there nothing preternatural in this? Was there no influence here from the powers of darkness?" — suggesting Satan may have had a hand in weighing down their eyes at the very moment they should have watched.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson from the source of the drowsiness, which Luke ascribes to sorrow: "this drowsiness arose neither from excessive eating and drinking, nor from gross stupidity... but rather from immoderate [grief]. Hence we perceive more clearly how strong is the tendency of our flesh to indifference; since even dangers lead us to forgetfulness of God." Satan finds his opportunity either way: "if we dread no danger, he intoxicates and drowns us in [security]; and if we experience fear and sorrow, which ought to arouse us to pray, he overwhelms our senses." Calvin marks the aggravation that "the disciples, after having been sharply reproved, almost at that very moment fall again asleep" — and not the whole body, but "the three whom Christ had selected to be his chief companions... the flower of them."

The witnesses agree: the repeated sleep of the chosen three, even after reproof, mirrors the weakness of all flesh, which neither danger nor grief can keep awake to God without his sustaining grace. Christ's repeated prayer, by contrast, teaches that "we must not be discouraged or grow weary in praying, if we do not immediately obtain our wishes" (Calvin) — the Master watching and praying while his disciples sleep.
44:
"And he left them, and went away again, and prayed the third time, saying the same words." The witnesses note the threefold prayer and the significance of its repetition. Gill observes he went "at some little distance from them; they being so overpowered with sleep, that he could have no conversation with them." Clarke draws the Pauline parallel: "So St. Paul — I besought the Lord thrice that it might depart from me (2 Corinthians 12:8). This thrice repeating the same petition argues deep earnestness of soul." Barnes simply cross-references the same.

Ellicott reflects on the nature of such repetition: "The fact is suggestive, as indicating that there is a repetition in prayer which indicates not formalism, but intensity of feeling. Lower forms of sorrow may, as it were, play with grief and vary the forms of its expression, but the deepest and sharpest agony is content to fall back upon the iteration of the self-same words." The threefold "same words" are thus not vain repetition but the mark of an anguish too deep for variation — the soul returning again and again to the one cry that holds its whole burden.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the cycle of Gethsemane prayers. The witnesses connect it to the warning Christ had given against the "vain repetitions" of the heathen (Matthew 6:7), and (as Calvin noted under v.43) distinguish the two sharply: the heathen "hope that they will obtain by idle talking what they do not ask honestly," whereas Christ's repetition flows from genuine, persevering earnestness. The lesson drawn is that "we must not be discouraged or grow weary in praying, if we do not immediately obtain our wishes... it is not a superfluous repetition of the words, if a repulse which we have experienced is so far from extinguishing the ardor of prayer, that we ask a third and fourth time what God appears to have denied" (Calvin, under v.43).

The threefold prayer, answered not by the cup's removal but by strength to drink it, becomes for the witnesses a pattern of importunate prayer that submits to the Father's will. Christ, who had taught his disciples to pray, here prays himself in the hour of deepest need, repeating the same surrendered petition until the struggle is won and he can rise to meet the betrayer with composure.
45:
"Sleep on now, and take your rest: behold, the hour is at hand, and the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners." The chief difficulty — the apparent contradiction with "Rise, let us be going" (v.46) — divides the witnesses. The most-cited solution is Augustine's, drawing on Mark's "it is enough" (Mark 14:41): "we clearly understand the Lord to have been silent some time after He had said, Sleep on, to allow of their doing so, and then after some interval to have roused them." Others read it as irony or a question. Barnes prefers the question: "Do you sleep now, and take your rest? Is this a time... to give yourselves to sleep?" — supported by Luke's "Why sleep ye?" Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson allow the ironic or "mournful irony" reading. Calvin is decisive for irony with a sting: "Having hitherto wasted my words on you, I shall now come to exhort you; but whatever permission I may give you to [rest], the enemies will not allow it to you, but will compel you to watch against your will."

The fathers add allegory and theology. Hilary reads the threefold finding-asleep as the Church's history — rebuked after the resurrection, visited by the Spirit, and at last given rest at Christ's glorious coming. Origen distinguishes this permitted rest from the earlier heavy-eyed sleep, allowing that "the soul, unable to sustain a continual energy... may blamelessly admit some relaxations." Chrysostom stresses the words "into the hands of sinners" show "this was the work of their wickedness, not that He was guilty of any crime," while "the hour is at hand" points to "Divine interference." Jerome: Christ "goes forth undaunted... and offers Himself voluntarily to be sacrificed."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices read the sudden change as marking the arrival of Judas. Ellicott: "it was not till after these words had been spoken that the Traitor and his companions were seen actually approaching." Robertson: "The Master's time of weakness is past; He is prepared to face the worst" — "He has now won the victory without their aid." Cyril of Jerusalem, here on-topic, stresses Christ "came to His passion willingly... lest the whole world should perish in its sins." The witnesses agree the verse marks the turning-point: the prayer is finished, the victory won, and the hour of betrayal has struck.
46:
"Rise, let us be going: behold, he is at hand that doth betray me." The witnesses read this not as a call to flight but to confront the betrayer with courage. Gill: "Not to run away from the enemy, but to meet him... he was now rid of his fears, and free from those agonies and dreadful apprehensions... and likewise, to signify his willingness to be apprehended, and to suffer, and die, in the room of his people." Barnes and Ellicott concur: "the latter clause does not involve any suggestion of flight, but rather a call to confront the danger." Clarke: "I go willingly to meet that death which their malice designs me, and, through it, provide for the life of the world." Robertson notes the verb "is at hand" (perfect of engizō) is the very word John the Baptist used of the kingdom (Matthew 3:2), and observes we cannot know "whether Jesus heard the approach... or saw the lights or just felt the proximity of the traitor."

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse the seal of Gethsemane's victory: "By these words he declares that, after having prayed, he was furnished with new arms. He had formerly... been sufficiently voluntary as to dying; but, when he came to the point, he had a hard struggle with the weakness of the flesh." Now, "by [prayer] (Hebrews 5:7) new strength from heaven," "the trembling is allayed, and the fear is subdued," so that he "not only does not retire or conceal himself, but cheerfully advances to death."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which is the hinge between the agony in the garden and the arrest. The witnesses agree on its decisive force: the same Jesus who, moments before, fell on his face pleading that the cup might pass, now rises with composure to walk straight toward his betrayer and his death. The contrast measures the completeness of the victory won in prayer. Wesley (under v.50) captures the tone the witnesses hear throughout: "With what composure does he go forth to meet the traitor!... plainly showing his superiority over them, and even then leading as it were captivity captive."

The "Rise, let us be going" is thus the voice of a conqueror, not a fugitive — Christ taking the initiative in his own passion, going voluntarily to lay down his life. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to chapter 5 on oaths, not this verse.)
47:
"And while he yet spake, lo, Judas, one of the twelve, came, and with him a great multitude with swords and staves." The witnesses dwell on the horror of the betrayal and the disproportionate force sent against the unarmed Christ. The repeated phrase "one of the twelve" carries the weight. Robertson: "The very horror of the thing is thus emphasized, that one of the chosen twelve apostles should do this dastardly deed." Clarke: "He who a little before had been one of the leaders of the flock of Christ is now become the leader of ruffians and murderers! What a terrible fall!"

The grammatical-historical voices detail the composition of the band. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, following John 18:3, identify it as including the Roman cohort (the "band") sent by Pilate to prevent a tumult, armed with swords, and the temple officers (Levites or Nethinim) with clubs, carrying "lanterns and torches" though it was near the Passover full moon. Ellicott notes they went to Gethsemane "as that was known to Judas as one at least of our Lord's chosen resorts" (John 18:2).

The puritan Henry (whose long exposition covers vv.47-56) draws the lessons: Christ "as a sacrifice for sins, stood thus abandoned... In this he was made a curse for us." The disciples' flight (v.56) "was their sin... There was unkindness in it... unfaithfulness in it," yet "a part of Christ's suffering... to be thus deserted." He marks the depth of Judas's crime: "No enemies are so much to be abhorred as those professed disciples that betray Christ with a kiss." The Reformed Calvin stresses the providence: "he was not dragged to death by external violence, except so far as wicked men carried into execution the secret purpose of God," and the very size of the force betrays their bad conscience — "the divine power of Christ, which they had been compelled to feel by numerous proofs, inwardly tormented them."

The Latin fathers lift it higher. Leo: "among all the works of God's mercy... none is more wondrous... than that Christ was crucified for the world," in which "the Catholic Faith maintains... the two Natures to have met in our Redeemer." Origen finds even now Jesus "betrayed into the hands of sinners" when "those who seem to believe in Jesus continue to sin while they have Him in their hands." The witnesses agree: the armed multitude, led by an apostle, comes against the Prince of life, who yet submits by the Father's secret purpose.
48:
"Now he that betrayed him gave them a sign, saying, Whomsoever I shall kiss, that same is he: hold him fast." The witnesses explain the necessity and the calculated treachery of the sign. Gill offers practical reasons it was needed: "it being night... Judas himself might not be able to discern him... moreover, Christ and his apostles might be clothed alike, so that a mistake might be easily made; and... the Roman soldiers... might never have seen him." Barnes adds that "Judas, therefore, being well acquainted with him, to prevent the possibility of mistake, agreed to designate him by one of the tokens of friendship," and recalls John's account that, when Jesus said "I am he," the band "went backward, and fell to the ground" (John 18:6).

Clarke is vehement at the cold premeditation: "How coolly deliberate is this dire apostate!... how deeply hypocritical!... how diabolically malicious!" He compares Judas to Joab, who "while he pretends to inquire tenderly for the health of Amasa, thrust him through with his sword; but the disciple here vastly outdoes his master." Robertson notes Mark's pluperfect "had given" shows the sign was arranged "before arrival at Gethsemane," and that Judas chose the kiss as a common greeting, intending to "kiss him fervently" (v.49).

The Reformed Calvin probes Judas's motive: "I have no doubt that Judas was restrained, either by reverence for our Lord, or by shame for his crime, from venturing openly to avow himself as one of the enemies." The warning "hold him fast" he conjectures arose because Judas "recollected the numerous proofs by which Christ had formerly attested his divine power." Yet it was "astonishing madness, either to attempt to conceal himself by frivolous hypocrisy, when he came into the presence of the Son of God, or to oppose the tricks and dexterity of men to his boundless power."

The witnesses agree: the sign of the kiss is the supreme perversion of a token of love into an instrument of betrayal. Judas, who knew Christ's power and had perhaps seen it overthrow his captors, still trusted his cynical stratagem — choosing the most intimate gesture of affection to mark out his Master for arrest. The detail "hold him fast" betrays both his fear of Christ's power and his determination not to let the prize escape, deepening the witnesses' sense of the calculated, deliberate wickedness of the act.
49:
"And forthwith he came to Jesus, and said, Hail, master; and kissed him." The witnesses dwell on the hypocrisy of the kiss and the title. Gill notes Judas "went before them... that it might not be thought that he came with them." Barnes explains "Hail" means "to rejoice... to have cause of joy," expressing "the joy which one friend has when he meets another, especially after an absence" — making Judas's use of it the more grotesque. "Master" is "Rabbi," and the kiss "the common salutation of friends." Lightfoot adds a telling cultural note: "It was not unusual for a master to kiss his disciple; but for a disciple to kiss his master was more rare. Whether therefore Judas did this under pretence of respect, or out of open contempt and derision, let it be inquired."

The Reformed Calvin reads it as feigned compassion: "Judas, as if trembling for his Master's danger, pretended by these words to have some feeling of compassion." Mark's repeated "Master, Master" he takes as the speech "of a man who spoke from strong feeling." Calvin discerns the diabolical self-deception: "though he was impressed with the majesty of Christ, still the devil so fascinated his mind, that he felt assured that his treachery was concealed." The kiss itself, since Judas "had left Christ but a little before," seems "the last [salute] to his Master" — "thus he excels the rest in the appearance of affection," yet "how little he gained by his deception is evident from Christ's reply."

Robertson (under v.48) had noted the intensive verb katephilēsen, "kissed him fervently," calling it "the most terrible instance of the profuse kisses of an enemy" (Proverbs 27:6) — the same verb used of the sinful woman (Luke 7:38), the father's embrace of the prodigal (Luke 15:20), and the Ephesian elders' farewell to Paul (Acts 20:37). The very warmth of the gesture deepens its treachery.

The witnesses agree: in the "Hail, master" and the fervent kiss, hypocrisy reaches its height. Judas, who had eaten at Christ's table and been numbered among the twelve, greets his Master with the warmest tokens of love at the very moment of handing him over to death. The witnesses see in it not merely betrayal but the self-deceiving conviction that the treachery might pass undetected — a deception that Christ's gentle, knowing reply (v.50) at once exposes.
50:
"And Jesus said unto him, Friend, wherefore art thou come?" The witnesses examine both the word "Friend" and the question. On "Friend," the grammatical-historical voices stress it is not the warm word for a beloved friend. Barnes: "In the Greek there are two words which our translators have rendered friend: one implying affection... the other not... It is this latter word," the same used for the disaffected labourer (Matthew 20:13) and the guest without a wedding garment (Matthew 22:12). Clarke renders it "companion," and Ellicott "comrade" or "mate," a term used "by fellow-soldiers, or sailors, of each other."

On the question, Robertson, following Deissmann, argues "wherefore art thou come" is better read as a sharp question: "Do your errand" or "why here" — Deissmann having shown from a first-century Syrian tablet that the phrase "was current coin in the language of the people." Barnes takes it as designed "to fill the mind of Judas with the consciousness of his crime, and by a striking question to compel him to think of what he was doing." Clarke imagines its sting: "Surely thou, who hast so long been my companion, art not come against me, thy Lord, Teacher and Friend!"

The Reformed Calvin reads it as a grave charge, not irony: Christ "charges him with ingratitude, that, from being an intimate who sat at his table, he had become a traitor, as had been predicted in the psalm" (Psalms 41:9; 55:12-14). The lesson is for the whole Church: "this evil, which Christ once sustained in his own person, is an evil to which the Church will always be exposed — that of cherishing traitors in her bosom." Therefore "we who are his disciples ought to worship God with sincerity... that no one give him a traitor's kiss."

Wesley admires Christ's bearing: "With what composure does he go forth to meet the traitor! With what calmness receive that malignant kiss! With what dignity does he deliver himself into the hands of his enemies!... even then leading as it were captivity captive." Gill and Clarke note the laying on of hands followed only after the band had been struck down by Christ's word (John 18:6), showing he submitted voluntarily. The witnesses agree: Christ meets betrayal with a word that is at once a reproach, an appeal to Judas's conscience, and a display of perfect composure — the Lord of all yielding willingly to his captors.
51:
"And, behold, one of them which were with Jesus stretched out his hand, and drew his sword, and struck a servant of the high priest's, and smote off his ear." The witnesses identify the swordsman as Peter (named only by John, 18:10, the others being silent for his protection), the servant as Malchus, and note Christ's healing of the ear (Luke 22:51). Why did the disciples have swords? Chrysostom and John Chrysostom explain from Luke 22:36-38: Christ had bidden them buy a sword, not to fight, but "to assure them that He was to be betrayed" — "indicating His being betrayed," not arming them. The two swords were likely "for the paschal lamb."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the detail. Barnes: the apostles "had provided two swords" (Luke 22:38), carried "in seasons of danger" against robbers. Robertson notes Peter "aimed to cut off the man's head, not his ear," and that Matthew, Mark, and Luke conceal Peter's name "for prudential reasons as he was still living." Clarke marks the healing as "another miracle, and striking proof of the Divinity of Christ," since to restore a severed ear required "unlimited power."

The fathers read it allegorically and morally. Jerome: Malchus, "one who should have been king," is made "the slave of the ungodliness of the Priests," losing his right ear "so that he might hear only the worthlessness of the letter." Hilary and Origen see in the cut ear the "disobedient hearing of a people which were the slaves of the Priesthood." Augustine notes Peter "used the sword... without the command or sanction of any superior or legitimate authority," yet sinned "not through hardened ferocity, but through a warmth of spirit capable of good," like Moses smiting the Egyptian. Leo: Christ "gives licence to their fury against Him, lest by putting off the triumph of His glorious Cross, the dominion of the Devil should be made longer."

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on rash zeal: "we are much more courageous and ready for fighting than for bearing the cross... Peter did not wait till he was commanded or permitted to strike, but inconsiderately proceeded to unlawful violence." The disciples' valor "is justly condemned" because they "improperly attempt more than the calling of God commands"; "no man shall move a finger, except so far as God commands." The witnesses agree: Peter's blow, though springing from love, was rash and unauthorized — and Christ's healing of the wound he caused both rebukes the violence and displays his mercy and power to his foes.
52:
"Then said Jesus unto him, Put up again thy sword into his place: for all they that take the sword shall perish with the sword." The witnesses debate the scope of this famous saying. The grammatical-historical Barnes offers three senses, preferring it as "a caution to Peter": his "unseasonable and imprudent defence might be the occasion of his own destruction," for "they who took the sword perished by it" — "probably a proverb." Clarke reads it both as a caution and "a prophetic declaration of the Jewish and Roman states," both of whom "took the sword against Christ, and both perished by it." Ellicott insists "the words... are obviously not a general rule declaring the unlawfulness of all warfare," but "limited in their range by the occasion": resistance then "would have been fighting not for God, but against Him." Yet he draws the inference: "In whatever other cause it may be lawful to use carnal weapons, it is not wise or right to draw the sword for Christ and His Truth" (2 Corinthians 10:4). Robertson: "Offensive war is here given flat condemnation... The sword calls for the sword."

The fathers distinguish lawful from unlawful use. Augustine: "he uses the sword who, without the command or sanction of any superior or legitimate authority, arms himself against man's life." Hilary notes not all literal sword-users die by it, so the meaning must be of those who use it "for the promotion of crime." Leo: it was "contrary to the sacrament of our redemption that He, who had come to die for all, should refuse to be apprehended." Remigius even allegorizes the "Angels" of v.53 as the Roman armies that would later avenge.

The Reformed Calvin gives the careful resolution. The saying "confirms the precept of the Law, which forbids private individuals to use the sword." He distinguishes "a civil court and the court of conscience": one who repels a robber "will not be liable to public punishment, because the laws arm him"; lawful self-defense requires that one "first lay aside excessive wrath, and hatred, and desire of revenge." But because "this scarcely ever happens, Christ properly reminds his people of the general rule, that they should entirely abstain from using [the sword]." Against the "fanatics" who would "wrest the sword out of the hands of judges," Calvin insists magistrates "are God's ministers" to whom this very power "is expressly ascribed." The witnesses agree the rebuke was first to Peter, condemning private violence — most extending it to a warning against defending Christ's cause by carnal weapons.
53:
"Thinkest thou that I cannot now pray to my Father, and he shall presently give me more than twelve legions of angels?" The witnesses read this as Christ's demonstration that he suffered voluntarily, having infinite power at his command. Gill notes it "must be understood of him as man," for as God "the angels were his creatures and ministering servants." Barnes: Peter's resistance "implied a distrust of the protection of God"; if rescue had been proper, "God could easily have furnished far more efficient aid" — "a mighty host of angels." A legion was "more than six thousand men," and "the number twelve was mentioned, perhaps, in reference to the number of his apostles" (one legion for each, Judas excepted). Clarke computes "more than 72,000" angels; Ellicott notes the number "is probably suggested by that of the Apostles. Not twelve weak men... but twelve legions of the armies of the Lord of Hosts." Robertson recalls "the story of Elisha at Dothan" (2 Kings 6:17).

Ellicott discerns the subtle theology: "There is a strange and suggestive blending of the possible and the impossible in these words. Could He have brought Himself to utter that prayer, it would have been answered. But He could not so pray unless He knew it to be in harmony with His Father's will, and He had been taught, in that hour of agony, that it was not." The "presently," he reminds us, means "immediately, without a moment's delay."

The Reformed Calvin resolves the apparent contradiction — that Christ could ask the Father to revoke the very decree appointing his death: "Christ speaks conditionally, that he has a far better method of defending his life, were it not that the will of [the Father] was opposed to it... he refrained from presenting such a request to [the Father], because he knew that it was contrary to his decree." From this he draws "a useful doctrine, that those who resort to unlawful means on the plea of necessity pour dishonor on God"; for "few look for the secret protection of God," forgetting the angels "placed as guardians" (Hebrews 1:14).

The witnesses agree: Christ's submission was not weakness but sovereign choice. With twelve legions of angels instantly available, he chose the cross — proving, as Chrysostom said (under v.51), that "it was voluntarily that He suffered." The verse rebukes Peter's faithless violence and consoles every believer that the seeming defeat of the arrest was, in truth, the willing self-surrender of the Lord of hosts to fulfil the Father's saving purpose.
54:
"But how then shall the scriptures be fulfilled, that thus it must be?" The witnesses read this as the deepest ground of Christ's submission — the necessity foretold in Scripture. Gill: had angels rescued him, "how then should the Scriptures, which speak of Christ's being taken, and led as a lamb to the slaughter... have their accomplishment?" Barnes: "the scriptures which foretold his dying for the world... must be accomplished," and Christ said this "to comfort his disciples; to show them that his death was not a matter of surprise or disappointment to him." Clarke points to the chief prophecies: "Psalm 22, 69, and especially Isaiah 53, and Daniel 9:24-27"; Christ "shows that they had no power against him but what he permitted; and that he willingly gave up himself into their hands."

Ellicott finds here "the source of the peace and calmness which had come to our Lord's human soul out of the depths of its agony. All that was sharpest and most bitter was part of a pre-ordained discipline. Not otherwise could the Scriptures be fulfilled, which had painted... the picture of the ideal Sufferer not less vividly than that of the ideal Conqueror and King. It was meet that He too should be made perfect through sufferings" (Hebrews 2:10). Robertson: "Jesus sees clearly his destiny now that he has won the victory in Gethsemane."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of Scripture's authority over the flesh: Christ "ought not to attempt any method of escaping death, to which he knew that he was called by the Father." Though for himself he needed no Scripture to know God's appointment, "because mortals do not know what God has determined to do until it be revealed by his word, Christ, with a view to his disciples, properly refers to the testimony which God gave of his will." The wider use: "Scripture is a sufficient bridle for subduing the rebellion of the flesh; because God points out to us what is his will for the very purpose of keeping us in subjection" (Romans 15:4).

The witnesses agree: the calm that settled on Christ after Gethsemane rested on the conviction that "thus it must be." His suffering was no accident or defeat but the fulfilment of the whole prophetic witness — Isaiah's suffering servant, the pierced one of the Psalms, the cut-off Messiah of Daniel. By submitting, Christ did not yield to superior force but obeyed the Father's revealed and necessary purpose, and so left his disciples (and all believers) the example of bowing to God's will as made known in his word, even when it leads through the cross.
55:
"In that same hour said Jesus to the multitudes, Are ye come out as against a thief with swords and staves for to take me? I sat daily with you teaching in the temple, and ye laid no hold on me." The witnesses read this as Christ's expostulation exposing the cowardice and injustice of the arrest. Ellicott and Robertson clarify the word: not "thief" but "robber" — the same word used of Barabbas (John 18:40), "the brigand chieftain of a lawless band." Robertson notes the irony: "He will be crucified between two robbers and on the very cross planned for their leader, Barabbas." Barnes: "It adds not a little to the depth of his humiliation, that he consented to be hunted down thus by wicked men, and to be treated as if he had been the worst of mankind."

The witnesses note the historical detail "I sat daily teaching in the temple." Ellicott observes the "sitting" implied "the position of a teacher more or less recognised," and that the statement "looks beyond" the recent days to "a ministry in Jerusalem, which their narratives pass over." Clarke: "Every day, for four days past, ye might have met with me in the temple." The Antiochene Chrysostom explains why they came by night: "they did not lay hands on Him in the temple because they feared the multitude," so "the Lord went forth that He might give them place and opportunity to take Him" — teaching that "if He had not suffered them of His own free choice, they would never have had strength to take Him."

The fathers add moral readings. Jerome: "It is folly then to seek with swords and staves Him who offers Himself to your hands." Origen and others note Peter "followed afar off," because "he was about to deny his Lord." The Reformed Calvin draws out the expostulation: "What necessity was there for making such a display of arms against me, as if your object had been to overcome some robber?" — while reminding them that, "though they had a traitor for their leader, they approached him with trembling," wounding "their evil conscience."

The witnesses agree: Christ's words lay bare the absurdity and malice of the armed nocturnal arrest of one who had taught openly and peaceably in the temple. The very manner of his seizing — by night, by stealth, with a band of soldiers against an unarmed teacher — was a confession of their own guilt and fear, and a fulfilment of the Scriptures that the Shepherd should be smitten and led as a lamb to the slaughter.
56:
"But all this was done, that the scriptures of the prophets might be fulfilled. Then all the disciples forsook him, and fled." The witnesses divide the verse between the divine purpose and the disciples' shameful flight. On the first clause, Gill, Barnes, and Ellicott (following Mark 14:49) take it as Christ's own words rather than the evangelist's comment: the betrayal and arrest were "all working out a divine purpose, and... fulfilling the Scriptures in which that purpose had been shadowed forth" (Ellicott), citing Genesis 3:15; Psalms 22; 69; Isaiah 53; Daniel 9:24-26; Zechariah 13:7 (Barnes). Calvin states the doctrine: "whatever may be the unbridled rage by which Satan and all ungodly men are actuated, still the hand of God always prevails, so as to draw them reluctantly wherever he pleases"; yet "since God does not employ them as his lawful ministers... they are not excusable."

On the flight, the witnesses lament and explain. Clarke: "Jesus is abandoned by all! — even zealous Peter and loving John are among the fugitives!... Was ever master so served by his scholars?" He turns it on the reader: "Are there not found among his disciples still... persons who abandon him, his people, his cause, and his truth?" Ellicott offers mitigation: "we may remember (1) the weariness and exhaustion which had overcome them... and (2) that they had been told not to resist, and that flight might seem to them the only alternative." The puritan Henry (under v.47) had called it "their sin... unkindness... unfaithfulness," yet also "a part of Christ's suffering," and a mystery: "Christ, as the Saviour of souls, stood thus alone... He trod the wine-press alone" (Isaiah 63:3).

Barnes and Ellicott both note Mark's strange detail of "a young man having a linen cloth cast about his naked body," who fled naked when seized — probably the owner of the garden or "a friend of Jesus" (Mark 14:51).

The witnesses agree: the flight of the disciples, who had vowed to die with him, fulfilled Christ's own prediction (v.31) and the prophecy of the smitten Shepherd whose sheep are scattered (Zechariah 13:7). It both deepened his suffering — to be utterly abandoned in his hour of need — and displayed that he wrought salvation alone, with no human aid. The remark "all this was done that the scriptures might be fulfilled" frames even this desertion within the sovereign purpose of God, so that nothing in the passion fell out by chance, but all by the eternal counsel foretold in the prophets.
57:
"And they that had laid hold on Jesus led him away to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and the elders were assembled." The witnesses set the scene of the night trial and note its illegality. John records that Jesus was first led to Annas, the father-in-law of Caiaphas; Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott explain this as a mark of respect to the former high priest and to allow the council to gather. Caiaphas "was high priest for that year" and "had prophesied that it was expedient that one should die for the people" (John 11:49-50) — already "committed to the policy of condemnation" (Ellicott).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the procedural violations: "It was against the rules of Jewish law to hold a session of the Sanhedrin for the trial of capital offences by night," still more on the Passover night. The gathering was therefore "an informal one — probably a packed meeting of those who were parties to the plot," Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea and others "not being summoned." Only at daybreak (Luke 22:66) did they "transform themselves into a formal court."

The puritan Henry (whose exposition covers vv.57-68) draws the lessons on Peter following "afar off" — "to follow Christ afar off, is to begin to go back from him" — and on Christ's open confession: "Hitherto Jesus had seldom professed expressly to be the Christ... but now he would not omit to make an open confession of it... as an example and encouragement to his followers, to confess him before men." The Reformed Calvin lifts the scene to its theological depth: "nowhere else than at Jerusalem was there at that time either a temple of God, or lawful worship, or the face of a Church... and yet all of them unite in conspiring to extinguish the only hope of salvation." Caiaphas "was a figure of the only Mediator," and those with him "represented the whole Church of God," yet they condemn the Redeemer — as foretold of the rejected cornerstone (Psalms 118:22) and the stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14).

The witnesses agree: the trial before Caiaphas was a travesty of justice — a hasty, illegal, packed night assembly of men already resolved on death, the visible Church of God conspiring against its own Head. Yet, as Calvin observes, "the Lord wisely made provision that such wickedness of men should not perplex believing souls," for it had all been foretold; and from the chains and condemnation of Christ flows, by God's design, the loosing of his people from the tyranny of sin.
58:
"But Peter followed him afar off unto the high priest's palace, and went in, and sat with the servants, to see the end." The witnesses read this as the fateful first step toward Peter's denial — courage and cowardice mingled. Gill: "Peter's following Christ, showed love to him... and yet keeping at a distance, following him afar off, betrayed fear." Barnes: it evinced "real attachment... a desire to be near him," yet also "fear respecting his personal safety. He therefore kept so far off as to be out of danger." He draws the application: "Many imitate Peter. They are afraid to follow the Saviour closely... so far that it is difficult to discern that they are in the train... We may measure our piety by our desire to be with him."

Clarke marks it "the beginning of his dreadful fall," and warns: "When a man is weak in faith, and can as yet only follow Christ at a distance, he should avoid all dangerous places, and the company of those who are most likely to prove a snare. Had not Peter got to the high priest's palace, and sat down with the servants, he would not thus have denied his Lord." Ellicott and Barnes, following John, note that Peter gained admission through "another disciple" (John, known to the high priest), and sat in the courtyard with the officers and slaves at a charcoal fire kindled "in the chill of the early dawn." Robertson: Peter was "more courageous than the rest and yet not courageous enough."

Ellicott probes the motive "to see the end": "It was, we may believe, more than a vague curiosity. There was something of sorrowful anxiety... but there was no fervent devotion, no prayer for himself or his Master, only the fevered restlessness of uncertain expectation, and so all the natural instability of his character had free play, with nothing to control it."

The witnesses agree: Peter's distant following and his sitting among the servants set the stage for his fall. Love drew him near enough to witness, fear kept him far enough to deny; and the want of watchful prayer (which Christ had urged, v.41) left his unstable nature exposed. The "warming himself" at the enemy's fire becomes, for the witnesses, an emblem of the believer who seeks comfort in the world's company and finds there only the occasion of denying his Lord. Wesley: "love constrained him to follow his Master; fear made him follow afar off... Unfit companions as the event showed."
59:
"Now the chief priests, and elders, and all the council, sought false witness against Jesus, to put him to death." The witnesses expose the trial as a foregone conclusion dressed in legal forms. The Antiochene Chrysostom: "this conventicle of ruffians sought to give their conspiracy the character of a legal trial. But it was entirely a scene of confusion and uproar... it was in truth nothing more than the anarchy of a den of robbers." The Alexandrian Origen finds in the failure of the witnesses "a great testimony in favour of Jesus, that He had lived and taught so irreproachably, that though they were many, and crafty, and wicked, they could find no semblance of fault in Him."

The grammatical-historical voices stress the perversion of justice. Clarke: "What a prostitution of justice! — they first resolve to ruin him, and then seek the proper means of effecting it." Ellicott notes "the tense of the Greek verb implies a continued process of seeking"; the law required "at least two witnesses" (Deuteronomy 17:6; 19:15) examined separately, and "the haste which marked all the proceedings had probably prevented previous concert," so the witnesses contradicted one another. Robertson: "Judges have no right to be prosecutors and least of all to seek after false witness and even to offer bribes to get it."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: "nothing was farther from the design of the priests than to inquire into the cause... For they had previously resolved to put Christ to death, and now they only seek a pretense for oppressing him. Now it is impossible that equity can have any place where an examination of the cause is not the first step." Yet from their very failure shines Christ's innocence: "amidst the darkness of their rage, the innocence of the Son of God shone so brightly, that the devils themselves might know that he died innocent."

The witnesses agree: the search for false witness reverses the whole order of justice — verdict first, evidence after. The council's inability to find even two agreeing false witnesses, despite their crafty malice, becomes the strongest testimony to Christ's spotless life. He who was condemned was demonstrably innocent; the judges, by their own corrupt proceedings, acquitted him even as they sought his death. The witnesses see in this both the depravity of his enemies and the providence of God, who would not allow his Son to be condemned on a true charge, that the world might know he suffered not for his own sin but for ours.
60:
"But found none: yea, though many false witnesses came, yet found they none. At the last came two false witnesses." The witnesses dwell on the repeated failure and its providential meaning. Gill: they "found none" that were "fit for their purpose." Barnes clarifies the council's intent: "We are not to suppose that they wished them to be false witnesses. They were indifferent... if they could succeed in condemning him"; but the witnesses "differed about facts, times, and circumstances, as all false witnesses do" (Mark 14:56, "their witness agreed not together"), and the Jews "did not dare to condemn him without conforming, in appearance at least, to the requirements of the law." Robertson: "They found false witnesses in plenty, but not the false witness that would stand any sort of test."

Clarke draws the lesson on falsehood's self-defeating nature: "It is the property of falsity to be ever inconsistent, and to contradict itself; therefore they could not find two consistent testimonies, without which the Jewish law did not permit any person to be put to death." He sees God's hand: "the hand of God was in this business: for the credit of Jesus, and the honor of the Christian religion, he would not permit him to be condemned on a false accusation; and, therefore, at last they were obliged to change their ground, and, to the eternal confusion of the unrighteous council, he is condemned on the very evidence of his own innocence, purity, and truth!"

The Reformed Calvin defines the term carefully: "the appellation of [false witnesses] is applied not to those who contrive a lie which had no foundation, but to those who calumniously pervert what was justly said, and turn it into a crime" — as with the temple-saying (John 2:19), where they "put a wrong interpretation on his words." From the triviality of the charge "we may readily infer how greatly the priests and scribes were blinded by their fury, since, without any pretext, they demand that Christ shall be put to death."

The witnesses agree: the collapse of the false testimony, even after many came forward, is a standing monument to Christ's innocence and to the providence that guarded his trial. Falsehood, by its nature inconsistent, could not produce the two agreeing witnesses the law required; and so the council, unable to convict him of any real or even plausibly-alleged crime, was driven at last to condemn him out of his own mouth (vv.63-65). The very failure of his accusers proclaims that the Lamb led to the slaughter was without blemish.
61:
"And said, This fellow said, I am able to destroy the temple of God, and to build it in three days." The witnesses identify this as a distortion of Christ's saying about his body. Barnes and Robertson trace it to John 2:19, "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up," which "he spake of the temple of his body" — perverted by the witnesses into a threat against the Jerusalem temple. Mark adds "but neither so did their witness agree together," so even this charge failed of the legal requirement.

Jerome (under v.59) had defined the falsity precisely: "A false witness is one who takes what is said in a different sense from that in which it was said." Christ's words "Destroy this temple" they changed to "I can destroy the Temple of God"; "He said, Destroy, not, I will destroy, because it is unlawful to lay hands on ourselves"; and "He said, And I will raise it up again," pointing to "a living and breathing temple," which they twisted to "build it again," applying it to the temple of stone. Clarke: "It is very easy, by means of a few small alterations, to render the most holy things and innocent persons odious to the world, and even to take away the life of the innocent."

Ellicott notes it "remarkable that the two Gospels which record the charge do not record the words in which it had its starting-point," and suggests "the second cleansing of the Temple (Matthew 21:12) had revived the memory of the first," bringing back the words spoken three years before (John 2:19). Even then, he adds, "the witnesses did not agree" — "there were still discrepancies as to time, place, and the exact words, that did not fit in with the established rules of evidence."

The witnesses agree: the only charge that gained any traction was a garbled version of a profound saying about Christ's death and resurrection. By a slight change — "destroy" to "I can destroy," the temple of his body to the temple of stone — the accusers turned a prophecy of his own resurrection into a seditious threat. Yet even this perversion could not secure two agreeing witnesses, so that the trial, having failed to convict him of any deed, was forced to turn from witnesses to the direct interrogation of the accused (vv.62-63). The distorted temple-saying remains, for the witnesses, an example of how holy words may be twisted into a capital charge by malice.
62:
"And the high priest arose, and said unto him, Answerest thou nothing? what is it which these witness against thee?" The witnesses read the high priest's question as a frustrated attempt to break Christ's silence and extract self-incrimination. Gill: "He rose up from his seat in great wrath... partly being vexed, that they could get no other and better testimony; and partly because of Christ's contemptuous silence." Barnes: Jesus "was silent. He knew that the evidence did not even appear to amount to anything worth a reply"; the high priest's question was "probably put in anger," for "they had utterly failed in their proof" and "had no way left... but to draw it from his own lips."

Ellicott reflects on the long silence: the question "implies a long-continued silence, while witness after witness were uttering their clumsy falsehoods." He recounts a scholar's testimony that at the Oberammergau Passion-play this silence "came upon him with a force which he had never felt before," and traces in it "a deliberate fulfilment of the prophecy of Isaiah 53:7," echoed in 1 Peter 2:23. The proper reading, he suggests, joins the clauses: "Answerest Thou nothing to what these witness against Thee?"

The Reformed Calvin exposes the high priest's insinuation: "Caiaphas triumphs over him on account of his silence, as if he was struck dumb by being vanquished... But it is an instance of extreme wickedness that he insinuates that Christ is not free from blame, because [the witnesses] speak against him." The unspoken argument is, "they would not have appeared against thee without a good reason" — as if Caiaphas "did not know that those [witnesses] had been procured by fraud." Christ "was again silent, not only because the objection was frivolous, but because, having been appointed to be a sacrifice, he had thrown aside all anxiety about defending himself."

The witnesses agree: Christ's silence before the false witnesses fulfilled the prophecy of the suffering servant who "opened not his mouth" (Isaiah 53:7), and confounded the high priest, who could neither convict him by the witnesses nor provoke him to self-defense. The silence was not the dumbness of a man overcome, as Caiaphas pretended, but the deliberate composure of one who, knowing himself appointed to die, refused to defend a life he had come to lay down. It was a silence that spoke — to his judges of their injustice, and to all generations of the Lamb led to the slaughter.
63:
"But Jesus held his peace. And the high priest answered and said unto him, I adjure thee by the living God, that thou tell us whether thou be the Christ, the Son of God." The witnesses note that Christ, silent before false witnesses, will answer a solemn adjuration about his identity. Gill: "Knowing it would signify nothing, whatever he should say, they being set upon his death... he quietly submits." But Clarke marks the turn: "To this solemn adjuration Christ immediately replies, because he is now called on, in the name of God, to bear another testimony to the truth... He felt no disposition to continue this silence, when questioned concerning a truth, for which he came into the world to shed his blood."

The grammatical-historical voices explain the adjuration's craft. Ellicott: it was "skilfully worded so as to force upon our Lord the alternative either of denying what indeed He was, or of making a confession which would be treated as blasphemy"; technically "an oath of compurgation" (Exodus 22:11; Numbers 5:19-22). Robertson: "Caiaphas put Jesus on oath in order to make him incriminate himself, a thing unlawful in Jewish jurisprudence... To refuse to answer would be tantamount to a denial." This shows, Robertson adds, that Christ "was not thinking of oaths in courts of justice when he prohibited profanity" (Matthew 5:34).

The Reformed Calvin probes Caiaphas's bad faith: he "examines the matter on oath, as if he had been prepared to yield as soon as it was fully ascertained; but all the while his whole mind is filled with a malicious hatred." He "ought first to have inquired" whether Christ truly was the Messiah, examining "the signs"; instead, "having already determined to put Christ to death, [he is] satisfied with this pretense of sacrilege." From the form of the question Calvin infers "that it was at that time common among the Jews to bestow on the Messiah the title of [Son of God]," learned "from the predictions of Scripture."

The witnesses agree: the adjuration was a trap, designed to make Christ condemn himself whichever way he answered. Yet Christ, who had kept silence before lies, would not deny the truth when solemnly charged in God's name to declare it — for to be the Christ, the Son of God, was the very truth he came to attest with his blood. His readiness to answer the oath, while refusing to defend himself against false witness, shows the witnesses that his silence was never cowardice but a holy discrimination: silent before calumny, he speaks before the question of his divine Sonship, knowing his confession will seal his death.
64:
"Jesus saith unto him, Thou hast said: nevertheless I say unto you, Hereafter shall ye see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven." The witnesses hail this as the decisive, deliberate confession — and a warning of reversed roles to come. Gill and Barnes note "Thou hast said" is "a form of assenting," equivalent to Mark's plain "I am." Robertson: "a Greek affirmative reply." But Christ adds more: drawing on Daniel 7:13 and Psalms 110:1, "He claims that the day will come when Jesus will be the Judge and Caiaphas the culprit."

Ellicott marks the verse's immense weight against any merely-human view of Christ: "No words in the whole Gospel records are more decisive against the views of those who would fain see in our Lord only a great moral teacher, like Socrates or Cakya Mouni. At the very crisis of His history, when denial would have saved His life, He asserts His claim to be much more than this." At such a moment, "when men stand face to face with seeming failure and with death, dreams and delusive claims for the most part melt away. Here claims that men have presumed to think delusive were strengthened and intensified... in the calmness of assured conviction."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the contrast of the two comings: Christ "affirms that he is [the Son of God], not for the purpose of avoiding death, but rather of inflaming the rage of his enemies against him." Though "now... almost annihilated, by his mean garb, he announces, that at the proper time he will at length come with royal majesty, that they may tremble before the Judge, whom they now refuse to acknowledge." The lesson is wide: "how comes it that wicked men are so much at their ease... but because they do not set a high value on the crucified Jesus? It is therefore necessary to remind them of a dreadful judgment." For believers it is comfort: "they may now with the eyes of hope look for Christ and patiently wait till he comes." Wesley: "Our Lord looked very unlike that person now! But nothing could be more awful, more majestic and becoming."

The witnesses agree: in the supreme crisis Christ openly confesses his Messiahship and divine Sonship, and turns the tables on his judges — the despised prisoner will be their Judge, seated at the right hand of Power, coming in the clouds. The confession was made knowing it would be called blasphemy and bring death; it was therefore no self-preserving claim but a testimony sealed by the cross, the firmest possible proof that Jesus is all that the Church confesses him to be. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to chapter 23.)
65:
"Then the high priest rent his clothes, saying, He hath spoken blasphemy; what further need have we of witnesses?" The witnesses note both the formal significance of the act and its irony. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot documents the rule: "When witnesses speak out the blasphemy which they heard, then all... are bound to rend their clothes... and do not sew them up again" (Sanhedrin). Ellicott: "The act was almost as much a formal sign of condemnation as the putting on of the black cap by an English judge." Barnes and Clarke note that, strictly, "it was not lawful for the high priest to rend his clothes" (Leviticus 10:6; 21:10) — though this referred to his priestly robes, and rending in cases of blasphemy was thought allowable (Wesley).

The witnesses observe the fatal assumption underlying the charge. Barnes: "he has, under oath, arrogated to himself what belongs to God... the only proper point of inquiry [was] whether he had not given evidence that he was the Messiah, and that therefore his claims were just. This point... they never examined. They assumed that he was an impostor." Robertson: "Now it would not be blasphemy for the real Messiah to make such a claim, but it was intolerable to admit that Jesus could be the Messiah." Clarke quotes Quesnel on the "false zeal, a mask of religion" by which men cry "heresy, blasphemy, and faction" to oppress the truth.

The Reformed Calvin exposes the hypocrisy: the rending "plainly shows how boldly and wickedly those who profanely despise God make false pretensions of zeal." It would have been praiseworthy to feel resentment at real blasphemy, "but while he refused to make inquiry, he contrived an unfounded charge." Yet "this treacherous hypocrite, while he assumed a character which did not belong to him, taught the servants of God with what severity of displeasure they ought to regard blasphemies," condemning "the shameful cowardice of those who are no more affected by an outrage on religion... than if they heard buffoons uttering their silly jokes."

The witnesses agree: the high priest's torn garment, the prescribed sign of horror at blasphemy, was here the gesture of a hypocrite who refused to weigh the very claim he condemned. The deepest irony, several note, is that by rending his robe Caiaphas unwittingly signified the end of his own priesthood (as Jerome said under v.59, "the Jews have lost the priestly glory"). Real blasphemy would indeed have warranted the act; but since Jesus was the Christ, the charge fell, and the torn robe became a witness against the judge, not the accused.
66:
"What think ye? They answered and said, He is guilty of death." The witnesses note the unanimous verdict and the form of the sentence. Gill: the high priest, "as being president of the court," puts the question, and they pronounce him "guilty of death." Barnes explains the phrase: "he is found guilty of a crime to which the law annexes death," the law being that of blasphemy (Leviticus 24:10-16), which "sentenced him... to death by stoning." Yet "the chief priests... were unwilling to excite a popular tumult by stoning him, and they therefore consulted to deliver him to the Romans to be crucified."

Ellicott notes the older English idiom: "In modern English the word guilty is almost always followed by the crime... In older use it was followed by the punishment which the man deserved" (Numbers 35:31). The decision "as far as the meeting went, was unanimous. Sentence was passed." But carrying it out "while the Roman governor was at Jerusalem, presented a difficulty," for "the Jews, or at least their rulers, ostentatiously disclaimed the power of punishing capital offences" (John 18:31). Robertson: "They took the vote though it was at night and they no longer had the power of death since the Romans took it away from them... It was unanimous save that Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus did not agree," being "probably absent and not even invited as being under suspicion."

Clarke marks the violation of all judicial order: "The judge becomes a party and accuser, and proceeds to the verdict without examining whether all the prophecies concerning the Messiah, and the innumerable miracles which he wrought, did not justify him. Examination and proof are the ruin of all calumnies."

The witnesses agree: the council, having extracted Christ's confession and pronounced it blasphemy, condemned him to death without any inquiry into the truth of his claim. The verdict was a foregone conclusion (v.59), now given the form of unanimity by a packed assembly. The very irregularities — the night session, the self-incriminating oath, the unexamined claim, the absence of friendly members — mark it as a travesty. And the council's awareness that they lacked the power to execute (John 18:31) already points forward to the next stage: the delivery of Jesus to Pilate, that the Gentiles too might have their part in the death of the Lord of glory, and that he might die the Roman death of the cross as the Scriptures foretold.
67:
"Then did they spit in his face, and buffeted him; and others smote him with the palms of their hands." The witnesses dwell on the indignity and on the evangelists' honesty in recording it. The Antiochene Chrysostom asks why they added mockery to the death sentence: "That thou mightest learn their intemperate spirit... having taken Him like a prey, they thus showed forth their intoxication, and gave full swing to their madness." He praises "the self-command of the disciples" who "relate these things... disguising nothing, nor being ashamed thereof, but rather accounting it very great glory... that the Lord of the universe should endure to suffer such things for us." He fulfils Isaiah: "On that face which the sea, when it saw it, had reverenced, from which the sun... turned away his rays, they did spit, and struck it with the palms of their hands."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the meaning of the acts and their prophetic fulfilment. Barnes: spitting "among the Jews, as among us, was significant of the highest contempt and insult" (Numbers 12:14; Isaiah 50:6; Job 30:10); "buffeted" means "they struck him with their fists." Clarke parses the Greek: kolaphizein is "to beat with the hand, the fingers being clenched," and rhapizō "to smite the cheek with the open hand." Both fasten on Isaiah 50:6: "I gave my cheek to the smiters... I hid not my face from shame and spitting." Clarke adds: "Jesus not only takes no revenge... but bears all with meekness, without even one word of reply."

Gill notes the rabbinic horror at spitting — "he that spits before, or in the presence of his master, is guilty of death, so nauseous and filthy was it accounted; and how much more must it be to spit in the face of anyone?" — and connects it to Isaiah 52:14, "his visage was more marred than any man's."

The witnesses agree: the spitting, buffeting, and smiting fulfilled the prophecies of the suffering servant (Isaiah 50:6; 52:14) and displayed both the unrestrained cruelty of his captors and the meekness of Christ, who answered their insults with silence. The fathers see glory in the very shame — that the Lord of all should bear such indignity for us; and Augustine (under v.59) reads the spitting as a figure of those who "reject His proffered grace," the buffeting of those who "prefer their own honour to Him." The face defiled by their spittle is, for the witnesses, the face that bore our shame to restore in us the disfigured image of God.
68:
"Saying, Prophesy unto us, thou Christ, Who is he that smote thee?" The witnesses read this as the mockery of Christ's prophetic office, made the more cruel by the blindfold (recorded by Mark and Luke) that hid the smiters. The grammatical-historical and puritan voices catch the derision. Henry (under v.67): "They made sport with his prophetical office. They had heard him called a prophet, and that he was famed for wonderful discoveries; this they upbraided him with, and pretended to make a trial of; as if the divine omniscience must stoop to a piece of children's play." He likens them to "Belshazzar's revels in the temple bowls" — those "who profanely jest with the scripture, and make themselves merry with holy things."

Barnes (under v.67) had explained the smiting "with the palms of their hands" as if "to prevent a person's speaking, or to evince abhorrence of what he had spoken." The taunt "Prophesy" was a sarcastic challenge: if he claimed to be the Christ and a prophet, let him name, sightless, the one who struck him. The Gloss (under v.59) noted it "is said in ridicule of His claim to be held as a Prophet by the people."

The deeper irony, the witnesses see, is that Christ was at that very moment fulfilling his own prophecy — his prediction that he would be "mocked, and spitefully entreated, and spitted on" (Luke 18:32) — and that the prophecy of Peter's denial (v.34) was being fulfilled in the courtyard below even as they mocked his prophetic gift. Calvin (under v.65) had drawn the lesson: "the object of all these expressions of contempt was, to show that nothing was more unlikely than that he should be a prince of prophets, who... was not able even to ward off [blows]. But this insolence was turned by the providence of God to a very different purpose; for the face of Christ, dishonored by [spitting and blows], has restored to us that image which had been disfigured, and almost effaced, by sin."

The witnesses agree: the mocking demand "Prophesy" was a blind man's jest that unwittingly proclaimed the truth it derided. He whom they challenged to prophesy was the very Prophet whose words were being fulfilled around them, and whose silent endurance of their scorn was itself foretold by Isaiah. The shame they heaped on his prophetic office became, by God's providence, the means of restoring the divine image in fallen humanity — the mocked Christ bearing our contempt to redeem us from it.
69:
"Now Peter sat without in the palace: and a damsel came unto him, saying, Thou also wast with Jesus of Galilee." The witnesses begin the account of Peter's denial, marking its setting and the smallness of the temptation that felled him. The grammatical-historical Ellicott harmonizes the three denials: the first at his entry, in answer to the doorkeeper (John 18:17); the second by the fire, to another damsel and bystanders; the third an hour later in the porch — with the hasty words "probably repeated more than once on each occasion." He probes the impulse: "it was probably shame not less than fear," the same feeling that had cried "Be it far from thee, Lord" (Matthew 16:22) — Peter "shrank from the taunts and ridicule which were sure to fall upon the followers of One whose career was ending in apparent failure."

Barnes and Robertson note Peter sat "without" the hall where the trial proceeded, with the servants at the fire, where "the light shone in his face." The puritan Henry (whose exposition spans vv.69-75) draws the warnings: "Bad company leads to sin: those who needlessly thrust themselves into it, may expect to be tempted and ensnared, as Peter... It is a great fault to be shy of Christ; and to dissemble our knowledge of him, when we are called to own him, is, in effect, to deny him." He notes Peter "fell into the sin by surprise, not as Judas, with design."

The fathers read it as a mirror of human frailty and a warning. The Reformed Calvin makes it "a bright mirror of our weakness... a doctrine which may be applied to the whole Church." He blames Peter's rashness in entering the hall at all: "having been warned that he would revolt, he ought rather to have concealed himself... Thus it frequently happens that believers, under an appearance of virtue, throw themselves within the reach of temptation." On the slightness of the assault: "any man, who is not supported by the hand of God, will instantly fall by a slight gale or the rustling of a falling leaf... terrified by a woman's voice, [Peter] immediately denies his Master."

The witnesses agree: Peter's fall began not with a powerful assault but with a servant-girl's remark, in a place he should never have entered, among company that could only ensnare him. The disciple who had drawn his sword against an armed band (v.51) now quails before an unarmed maid — proof that human courage, however bold, is nothing without the sustaining grace of God, and that the want of watchful prayer (v.41) leaves even the most confident exposed to a shameful fall.
70:
"But he denied before them all, saying, I know not what thou sayest." The witnesses mark the public aggravation and the nature of the denial. Gill: "Which was a very great aggravation of his sin; for, as it is to a man's commendation to profess a good profession of Christ before many witnesses, so it is to his disgrace... to deny him before men." He specifies what Peter denied: "he did not deny that Christ was God, or the Son of God... but that he was with him, or one of his disciples." Barnes: "he denied that he was a disciple; he denied that he knew Jesus; he denied (Mark) that he understood what was meant... All this was palpable falsehood." Robertson: "I know not what thou sayest" was "an affectation of extreme ignorance that deceived no one... an easy and ancient dodge."

Clarke marks the progression: "Before, he followed at a distance, now he denies; this is the second gradation in his fall." The witnesses trace the descent step by step: distant following (v.58), denial (v.70), denial with an oath (v.72), denial with cursing and swearing (v.74).

The Reformed Calvin presses the public dimension: "he did not even dread a multitude of witnesses... the Spirit intended expressly to state this, that even the presence of men may excite us to hold fast the confession of faith." A public denial does public harm: "if we deny Christ before the weak, they are shaken by our example, and give way; and thus we destroy as many souls as we can." He weighs the sin precisely: Peter "does not absolutely [deny] the whole doctrine of the Gospel; he only [denies] that he [knew Christ]; but, because in the person of Christ he indirectly buries the light of the promised redemption, he is charged with base and shameful treachery." The lesson against evasion: "the wretched sophists, who endeavor to escape by ambiguous expressions... gain nothing by their dexterity in fraud," for "as soon as we depart from a plain and candid profession of Christ, we deprive him of the testimony to which he has a lawful claim."

The witnesses agree: Peter's first denial, made "before them all," was a public falsehood that both wronged Christ and endangered the weak. The feigned ignorance — "I know not what thou sayest" — was a transparent evasion, the first rung of a descent that would grow worse with each repetition.
