The witnesses see in it the warning that the confession of Christ must be plain and candid, especially before men, since to bury his name even by indirect denial is to rob him of the witness due him and to wound the faith of others.
71:
"And when he was gone out into the porch, another maid saw him, and said unto them that were there, This fellow was also with Jesus of Nazareth." The witnesses note Peter's restless movement and the second accusation. Gill: "he did not attempt to go out at the door, and run away... He chose rather to keep his ground, but was very uneasy; and therefore moved into the porch, where he sat very pensive." Barnes: "Peter was embarrassed and confused by the question; and to save his confusion attracting notice, he went away from the fire into the porch, where he expected to be unobserved. Yet in vain." Henry (under v.69) had noted the tragedy of this half-measure: "He went out into the porch before; and if he had gone quite off then, his second and third denial had been prevented; but then he came in again."

Barnes harmonizes the accusers: the charge was "repeated" by both a maid (Matthew, Mark) and a man (Luke) — "the truth probably is, that both were done." Robertson: "Peter was not safe out here, for another maid recognized him and spoke of him as this fellow with a gesture to those out there." Mark adds that "while in the porch... the cock crew," the first crowing.

The Reformed Calvin reconciles the Gospels: "it is probable that the statement which proceeded from one maid, flew from the lips of one to those of another, so that the first maid pointed him out to many persons and at several times." John even has "a multitude of men" attack Peter the second time, showing "the word, which had been pronounced by the [maid], was caught by the men standing by." On Mark's first cock-crow, Calvin explains that Christ's prediction meant "the [cock-crowing], which includes many [crowings]," so all the Evangelists agree that Peter thrice denied before the cock-crowing was ended.

The witnesses agree: Peter's move to the porch, meant to escape notice, only exposed him to a fresh accusation, and his refusal to flee outright kept him in the snare. His restlessness — embarrassed, uneasy, edging away yet not departing — is, for the witnesses, the very image of a soul caught between love and fear, unwilling either to confess Christ boldly or to leave the dangerous place. The opportunity to escape, had he taken it after the first denial, would have spared him the worse falls; but lingering, he was drawn deeper, until the second denial (v.72) followed the first, and the cock's first crow sounded unheeded.
72:
"And again he denied with an oath, I do not know the man." The witnesses mark the deepening of the sin — denial now reinforced by an oath. Gill: "he swore by the God of truth; made a solemn appeal to the omniscient God, the searcher of hearts, that he was so far from being ever with Jesus of Nazareth, or a disciple of his." Clarke names it "a third gradation of his iniquity. He has told a lie, and he swears to support it. A liar has always some suspicion that his testimony is not credited... and is therefore naturally led to support his assertions by oaths." Ellicott: "The downward step once taken, the disciple's fall was fatally rapid. Forgetful of his Lord's command forbidding any use of oaths in common speech (Matthew 5:34), he did not shrink from invoking the divine name... to attest his falsehood." Robertson notes Peter "even refers to Jesus as the man, an expression that could convey contempt, the fellow."

The Reformed Calvin draws the law of descending sin: "a sinner, after having once fallen, is always hurried on from bad to worse; so that those who begin with ordinary offenses afterwards rush headlong into the basest crimes, from which at first they would have recoiled with horror. And this is the just vengeance of God, after we have deprived ourselves of the assistance of the Holy Spirit, to allow Satan a violent exercise of power over us." This happens "chiefly in a denial of the faith; for when a man, through fear of the cross, has turned aside from a pure profession of the gospel, if he perceive that his enemies are not yet satisfied, [Satan] will proceed farther."

Yet Calvin also marks God's restraining mercy: "the Lord spared him by restraining the tongues of enemies from making additional attacks upon him," for had more pressed him, "he would have been ready to [deny] a hundred or a thousand times. It is every day necessary for the Lord to bridle Satan, lest he overwhelm us with innumerable temptations." Wesley adds soberly that Peter "denied with an oath — to which possibly he was not unaccustomed, before our Lord called him."

The witnesses agree: the second denial, sealed with an oath, shows how a single fall, unrepented, hurries the soul downward. Peter, who could not confess Christ before a maid, now invokes God's name to deny he ever knew "the man." The witnesses read in it both the just judgment of God, who withdraws the Spirit's help from those who deny their Lord, and his restraining mercy, who limits the assault lest the fallen disciple be utterly overwhelmed — preparing already, by that mercy, the way for Peter's recovery.
73:
"And after a while came unto him they that stood by, and said to Peter, Surely thou also art one of them; for thy speech bewrayeth thee." The witnesses note the third accusation, prompted by Peter's Galilean accent. Barnes (and Mark) explain: "Thy speech makes it manifest that you are of his company... he was a Galilean; and in this way his speech betrayed him." The Galileans "were distinguished for some peculiarity of pronunciation, perhaps some peculiar rusticity... that distinguished them from the refinement of the capital." Clarke and Ellicott detail the dialect: the Galileans "had a very corrupt pronunciation, frequently interchanging" the gutturals, "so blending or dividing words as to render them unintelligible," and changing "Sh into Th." Ellicott: "The Galilean patois was probably stronger when he spoke under the influence of strong excitement."

Barnes notes the interval — "about an hour after" (Luke 22:59) — and that Peter "by this time had returned into the palace or hall, and stood warming himself by the fire" (John 18:25). John adds the sharpest accusation: "a kinsman of Malchus, the servant whose ear Peter cut off, declared that he had seen him in the garden" (John 18:26). Robertson: "the little while was about an hour... the bystanders came up to Peter and bluntly assert that he was of a truth one of the followers of Jesus."

Ellicott reads the psychology: "The half-detection which the remark implied, perhaps also some sense of shame at the provincialism attracting notice, led to the more vehement denial that followed." Wesley observes God's providence in the weakness of the evidence: "Malchus might have brought a stronger proof than this. But such is the overruling providence of God, that the world, in the height of their zeal, commonly catch hold of the very weakest of all arguments against the children of God."

The witnesses agree: Peter's own tongue betrayed him. The very accent that marked him as a Galilean, a countryman of Jesus, exposed his hidden identity despite his denials. The third charge, pressed by the bystanders and (per John) by a kinsman of the man he had wounded, drove him to his most vehement denial yet. The witnesses see in it a quiet irony — that the disciple who had denied all knowledge of his Master was given away by the very speech of his homeland, so that flight and falsehood could not conceal what he was. The corner was now closed; only the cursing denial and the cock's crow remained (vv.74-75).
74:
"Then began he to curse and to swear, saying, I know not the man. And immediately the cock crew." The witnesses mark the depth of the third denial and the sudden signal of grace. Gill is careful about the cursing: "he cursed himself; he made dreadful imprecations and wishes; wished that all the miseries and calamities he could think of might fall upon him, if he was one of the disciples of Jesus" — not, he insists, cursing Christ, "for in so doing he would have sinned the sin against the Holy Ghost." Barnes: "irritated beyond endurance... he accordingly added to the sin of denying his Lord, the deep aggravation of profane cursing and swearing." Clarke: "This makes the fourth and final gradation in the climax of Peter's fall. From these awful beginnings it is not unfair to conclude that Peter might have gone almost as far as Judas himself, had not the traitorous business been effected before."

Robertson explains the cursing: "He called down on himself imprecations in his desperate irritation and loss of self-control at his exposure." Ellicott infers "some common formula of execration, such as God do so to me and more also (1 Kings 19:2)." On the cock-crow, Ellicott notes the Greek has no article — "a cock crew" — and that "cock-crowing had become a familiar phrase, as with us, for the earliest hour of dawn."

The Reformed Calvin marks the climax and its limit: "In this third denial, Peter's unfaithfulness reached its utmost height. Not satisfied with [an oath], he breaks out into [a curse], by which he abandons his body and soul to destruction." Yet "this passage shows that when a man, falling through weakness of the flesh, denies the truth though he knows it, this does not amount to blasphemy against the Holy Spirit" (Matthew 12:31-32); Peter "sinned through weakness and not through incurable malice." Clarke draws the wonder: "the great Apostle Peter fell through fear of a servant maid, and rose through the crowing of a cock."

The witnesses agree: Peter's fall reached its nadir in cursing and swearing his ignorance of "the man," and at that very moment the cock crew — the appointed sign Christ had foretold (v.34). The contrast is stark: the disciple at his worst, and the gracious signal that would, by recalling his Master's word, break his heart to repentance (v.75). The witnesses see in the cock-crow a token that even in the depths of his sin Peter was not abandoned; God had set the means of his recovery in the very prediction of his fall, so that the bird's cry became "instead of a John Baptist, the voice of one calling to repentance" (Henry).
75:
"And Peter remembered the word of Jesus... And he went out, and wept bitterly." The witnesses close the denial with the turn to repentance, brought about by Christ's word and, as Luke records, his look. Barnes recounts it movingly: at the cock-crow "Jesus turned and looked upon Peter... By a tender and compassionate look — a single glance of his eye — the injured Saviour brought to remembrance all Peter's promises, his own predictions, and the great guilt of the disciple," and Peter "flew from the palace... and wept bitterly." Ellicott: "that glance, full of tenderest pity and deepest sadness, as of one who was moved not by anger but by sorrow, recalled him to his better self, and the flood-gates of penitence were opened."

Barnes draws eight lessons: the danger of self-confidence ("Let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall"); that high privileges do not secure from falling; that sin's downward course is easy; that "true repentance is deep, thorough, bitter"; that a look from Jesus produces godly sorrow; that we should retire to solitude with our grief; that "real Christians may be suffered to go far astray" to teach them their weakness — for Peter's faith did not fail (Luke 22:32), only his courage; and that none may presume to sin under expectation of repenting, for "he that resolves to sin under the expectation of repenting hereafter, cannot be a Christian." He marks the evangelists' honesty in recording the fall, especially Mark's full account "written under Peter's direction."

The puritan Henry (under v.74) had drawn out the repentance: "The crowing of a cock is to Peter instead of a John Baptist, the voice of one calling to repentance. Conscience should be to us as the crowing of the cock." His sorrow "was secret... was serious; he wept bitterly... Those that have sinned sweetly must weep bitterly." Peter "who wept so bitterly for denying Christ, never denied him again, but confessed him often and openly." Clarke: "He went out — he left the place where he had sinned, and the company which had been the occasion of his transgression."

The witnesses agree: the word remembered and the look received broke Peter's heart, turning his fall into the occasion of a deep and saving repentance. Ellicott notes that the record of his fall "appears in every Gospel," indicating "that in after years he did not shrink from letting men know of his guilt, but sought rather that men might find in him a proof of the mercy and tender pity of his Lord" (cf. 1 Timothy 1:12-16). Where Judas's remorse ended in despair, Peter's penitence ended in restoration — the bitter tears of a true disciple who, fallen through weakness, was raised again by the grace of the Saviour he had denied.
